title: VOYAGER: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
one_line: VOYAGER is a GPT-4-driven lifelong-learning Minecraft agent that builds an ever-growing library of reusable executable-code "skills" via an automatic curriculum and iterative self-verification, outperforming prior LLM-agent baselines on exploration, tech-tree progression, and generalization to new worlds.
key_results: Discovers 63 unique items in 160 iterations = 3.3x more unique items than baselines; travels 2.3x longer distances; unlocks tech-tree milestones up to 15.3x faster (wooden 15.3x, stone 8.5x, iron 6.4x) and is the only method to unlock the diamond tool (1/3 trials, 102 iters). Iron tool: VOYAGER 21±7 iters vs AutoGPT 135±103 (ReAct/Reflexion 0/3). Zero-shot new-world tasks: VOYAGER solves all 4 tasks 3/3 (e.g., diamond pickaxe 19±3 iters) while ReAct/Reflexion/AutoGPT solve 0/3; skill library also boosts AutoGPT. Ablations: random curriculum -93% items, removing self-verification -73% items, GPT-3.5 code gen yields 5.7x fewer items than GPT-4.
how_it_relates: This is the archetypal "LLM writes reusable skills" self-evolving agent, so it is the central test case for the review's question. Critically, the paper itself frames the agent as harnessing "the world knowledge encapsulated in pre-trained LLMs" and "the internet-scale knowledge contained within GPT-4"; the "novelty" it demonstrates is discovery of items/behaviors that are novel to the agent within a fixed, human-designed game (Minecraft), not the invention of concepts absent from the training distribution. The skill library is essentially learned in-context orchestration/retrieval of code that GPT-4 can already produce, and the ablation showing GPT-3.5 gets 5.7x fewer items confirms performance is bounded by the base model's latent coding knowledge. This strongly supports the "elicitation" side: self-evolving here compounds and better exploits latent LLM knowledge rather than creating genuinely new ideas. It offers no metric of conceptual novelty relative to the training corpus.
datasets: Minecraft via MineDojo open-source framework (simulation environment)
Mineflayer JavaScript APIs for motor control
No offline training dataset; uses OpenAI APIs gpt-4-0314, gpt-3.5-turbo-0301, text-embedding-ada-002
baselines: ReAct
Reflexion
AutoGPT (re-implemented with GPT-4 for MineDojo)
AutoGPT w/ VOYAGER's skill library (for generalization)
VOYAGER w/o Skill Library (ablation)
VOYAGER w/ GPT-3.5 instead of GPT-4 (ablation)
metrics: Number of unique/novel items discovered (proxy for novelty/exploration)
Tech-tree milestone unlock speed in prompting iterations (wooden/stone/iron/diamond tool)
Map traversal distance
Zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks in a new world (success out of 3 trials, iterations to solve)
Ablation: % drop in discovered item count
limitations: High GPT-4 cost (15x GPT-3.5) and dependence on GPT-4's code-generation "quantum leap" that GPT-3.5/open-source LLMs cannot provide. Agent sometimes gets stuck failing to generate a correct skill; self-verification can misfire (e.g., not recognizing spider string as success). Hallucinations: curriculum proposes unachievable/nonexistent items (e.g. "copper sword," "copper chestplate"), GPT-4 misuses invalid fuels (cobblestone) or calls nonexistent API functions. No visual perception (text-only GPT-4 at the time); 3D building requires human feedback. Notably, no evaluation of novelty relative to human knowledge — "novelty" is confined to items within a pre-existing game.
code: https://voyager.minedojo.org (project page; code released, per project site)
evidence: The paper's own language ("harness the world knowledge encapsulated in pre-trained LLMs," "capitalizes on the internet-scale knowledge contained within GPT-4," "in-context form of novelty search") frames the method as eliciting/orchestrating latent LLM knowledge, not generating knowledge outside it. "Novelty" is operationalized purely as count of unique Minecraft items and tech-tree milestones the agent has not previously obtained within a pre-existing human-designed game with a fixed tech tree — i.e. novelty to the agent, not to human knowledge. No mechanism produces concepts absent from GPT-4's training distribution: hallucinations (proposing nonexistent "copper sword") are noted as failures, underscoring that the agent operates within, not beyond, learned game knowledge. The GPT-3.5-vs-GPT-4 ablation (5.7x fewer items with the weaker model) shows capability ceiling is set by the base model's pretrained knowledge, and the skill library is a plug-and-play retrieval asset (it also boosts AutoGPT), i.e. better exploitation of existing model competence rather than new discovery.
quotes: Recent advances in large language model (LLM) based agents harness the world knowledge encapsulated in pre-trained LLMs to generate consistent action plans or executable policies.
Our automatic curriculum capitalizes on the internet-scale knowledge contained within GPT-4 by prompting it to provide a steady stream of new tasks or challenges.
This approach can be perceived as an in-context form of novelty search.
contribution: First LLM-powered embodied lifelong-learning agent that (1) uses GPT-4 as an automatic curriculum for open-ended exploration, (2) maintains an ever-growing skill library of executable JavaScript programs indexed by embedding of their descriptions for retrieval/composition, and (3) introduces an iterative prompting mechanism combining environment feedback, execution errors, and GPT-4 self-verification. All done via blackbox GPT-4 queries with no gradient training/finetuning.
methodology: GPT-4 is prompted with the agent's state, exploration progress, and a directive to "discover as many diverse things as possible" to propose the next task (an in-context form of novelty search). For each task, GPT-3.5 generates a solving suggestion used to retrieve top-5 relevant prior skills from a vector-DB skill library; GPT-4 then writes code (with chain-of-thought), which is executed in a MineDojo/Mineflayer environment. Environment feedback, execution errors, and a separate GPT-4 self-verification critic iteratively refine the code (up to 4 rounds) until success, at which point the program is committed as a new reusable skill keyed by its description embedding. Skills are compositional (complex behaviors built from simpler stored programs), mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Self-Evolving AI Agents: A New Paradigm Bridging Foundation Models and Lifelong Agentic Systems
one_line: A survey that defines the "self-evolving AI agent" paradigm, proposes a unified four-component feedback-loop framework (System Inputs, Agent System, Environment, Optimiser) and a MOP→MOA→MAO→MASE trajectory, and catalogs optimisation techniques for LLM/prompts/memory/tools/workflows plus evaluation, safety, and open problems.
key_results: No empirical headline numbers — it is a survey with no experiments. The only quantitative claims are secondhand qualitative summaries of cited works (e.g., CACTUS "achieves significantly better performance than agents without tool integration"; Agent-as-a-Judge on DevAI "aligned more closely with human expert judgments" and reduced evaluation cost/time). No measurement of genuine novel-idea creation is reported.
how_it_relates: Directly central: the survey frames self-evolution explicitly as OPTIMISATION of existing agent scaffolding (prompts, memory, tools, topologies) around a fixed or lightly-adapted foundation model, guided by proxy metrics — i.e., the machinery it describes is fundamentally elicitation/configuration search rather than generation of new knowledge. It never defines or measures a "novelty" or "discovery" metric; evaluation is task-success, correctness, generalisation, and safety. It aspirationally invokes autonomous scientific discovery (generating hypotheses, designing experiments) as a future vision, but presents no mechanism or metric for verifying that evolved agents produce genuinely new ideas versus better-exploited latent capability. Its Law II ("Excel — preserve or enhance existing task performance") reveals the operative success criterion is performance on predefined tasks, not invention.
datasets: Survey — no experiments of its own. Benchmarks it catalogs include: ToolBench, API-Bank, MetaTool, ToolQA, GTA, AppWorld (tool/API agents)
BrowseComp, MM-BrowseComp, WebArena, VisualWebArena, WebCanvas, WebWalker, AgentBench (web agents)
MultiAgentBench, SwarmBench, GAIA (multi-agent/generalist)
Mobile-Bench, AndroidWorld, CRAB, GUI-World, OSWorld (GUI/multimodal)
SWE-bench, DataSciBench, MLGym, WorkBench, OpenAGI, SUPER, AgentClinic, MMedAgent, DevAI (domain-specific)
Safety: AgentHarm, RedCode, MobileSafetyBench, MACHIAVELLI, R-Judge, SafeLawBench, AgentEval
baselines: Not applicable — survey runs no experiments and reports no head-to-head baselines. It contrasts paradigms conceptually (MOP vs MOA vs MAO vs MASE) and mentions example method comparisons drawn from cited works (e.g., CACTUS with tool integration reported to beat agents without tools).
metrics: Task success rate
Accuracy / F1
Test pass rate (code)
Sub-goal / process-oriented completion
Policy/constraint adherence
LLM-as-a-Judge and Agent-as-a-Judge scores (pointwise, pairwise)
Safety/harm-compliance and robustness audits
NO novelty/discovery/creativity metric is defined or used
limitations: The survey itself flags: current systems are far from open-ended self-evolution; reward models for intermediate steps suffer scarcity/noise/instability; optimised prompts and topologies are brittle and transfer poorly across LLM backbones; reliable ground truth is often absent in scientific/legal domains, making trustworthy feedback signals hard; most optimisation is text-only lacking world models; tool creation and autonomous tool co-evolution are unsolved; and evaluation is mostly snapshot-based rather than longitudinal. For the core question, the key limitation is that no method or metric is offered to distinguish genuine discovery from performance gains via better elicitation.
code: https://github.com/EvoAgentX/Awesome-Self-Evolving-Agents (paper list); the framework EvoAgentX (Wang et al., 2025i) is cited as the first open-source implementation of the MASE loop
evidence: The paper's own taxonomy equates the concrete, currently-realisable content of "self-evolution" with optimisation of prompts, memory, tools, and topologies driven by proxy metrics/meta-rewards — this is the elicitation/prompt-optimisation side. It states current systems are "still far from" open-ended self-evolution and that progress "is achieved through agent evolution and optimisation techniques" (Sec 1). The Optimiser search spaces listed (prompt templates, tool selection, LLM parameters, architecture) and objectives (accuracy, F1, success rate, LLM-judge scores) are all exploitation of existing capability against fixed targets. Genuine novelty/discovery appears ONLY as future vision ("could autonomously generate hypotheses, design experiments") with no metric, mechanism, or evidence; the survey identifies "tool use and creation" and "open-ended self-evolution" as unsolved open challenges, implicitly conceding that current methods do not create genuinely new capabilities. No benchmark for novelty/creativity/discovery is proposed; a cited "Creativity in LLM-based multi-agent systems" survey (Chen 2025) is only referenced, not adopted.
quotes: Current progress towards this vision is achieved through agent evolution and optimisation techniques, which provide practical means for enabling agent systems to iteratively refine their components based on interaction data and environmental feedback.

MASE introduces a lifelong, self-evolving loop where a population of agents continually refines their prompts, memory, tool-use strategies and even their interaction patterns based on environmental feedback and meta-rewards.
In scientific discovery, self-evolving agent ecosystems could autonomously generate hypotheses, design experiments, and iterate on research workflows.
contribution: Formalises "Three Laws of Self-Evolving AI Agents" (Endure/Excel/Evolve); introduces a unified conceptual framework abstracting the self-evolution feedback loop into System Inputs, Agent System, Environment, and Optimiser; maps a paradigm shift from Model Offline Pretraining (MOP) to Model Online Adaptation (MOA) to Multi-Agent Orchestration (MAO) to Multi-Agent Self-Evolving (MASE); systematically reviews single-agent, multi-agent, and domain-specific (biomedicine, coding, finance, law) evolution/optimisation techniques; and surveys evaluation, safety/alignment, and open challenges.
methodology: This is a literature survey, not an empirical paper. Its "method" is a taxonomy: it abstracts existing agent-optimisation methods into an iterative closed-loop where an Optimiser (rule-based heuristics, gradient descent, Bayesian/MCTS/RL, or learning-based policies) updates an agent system's search space (prompt templates, tool selection, LLM parameters, architecture/topology) using feedback (proxy metrics like accuracy/F1/success-rate or LLM-based evaluators) from an environment, over System Inputs specified at task-level ({task description, D_train}) or instance-level ({x, y, C}). It groups techniques by which component they optimise and by domain, and organises open problems under the Endure/Excel/Evolve laws.

title: Agent Skills for Large Language Models: Architecture, Acquisition, Security, and the Path Forward
one_line: A survey of the emerging "agent skills" abstraction layer (SKILL.md packages, progressive disclosure, MCP integration), covering skill architecture, acquisition methods (human-authored vs. RL/autonomous), CUA deployment benchmarks, and security, plus a proposed trust/governance framework.
key_results: SAGE on AppWorld: 72.0% Task Goal Completion, 60.7% Scenario Goal Completion, +8.9% absolute over GRPO baseline, with 26% fewer steps and 59% fewer tokens. SEAgent on 5 novel OSWorld apps: 11.3%→34.5% success (+23.2 pp over UI-TARS). CUA-Skill on WindowsAgentArena: 57.5% SOTA. Agentic Proposing: 91.6% on AIME 2025 with a 30B solver. UI-TARS-2: 47.5% OSWorld, 73.3% AndroidWorld. Yuan et al.: 7B model 47.3% on ScreenSpot-Pro, +24.2 pp over 72B UI-TARS with only 3,000 samples. OSWorld-V reaches published-human parity (72.6% vs 72.36%). Security: 26.1% of 42,447 community skills have >=1 vulnerability; 5.2% high-severity; scripted skills 2.12x riskier; one actor accounted for 54.1% of confirmed malicious cases. Advanced Tool Use: up to 85% token-overhead reduction; programmatic tool calling 79.5%→88.1% on Opus 4.5.
how_it_relates: Directly frames skills as procedural-knowledge packaging, NOT as a mechanism for generating new knowledge. The paper explicitly positions the skill paradigm as the successor to prompt engineering and tool use — i.e., a better way to elicit and organize what models can already do, injecting "procedural knowledge" that "reshapes what the agent knows and can do before it generates its response." A skill "is not a model or a prompt, but a self-contained package" that "modifies the agent's preparation, not its output directly." This is squarely on the elicitation side: skills package/route latent capability rather than invent it. The one hint toward novelty is compositional synthesis (Agentic Proposing) yielding "capabilities that exceed what any individual skill provides," but that is composition of existing skills, not genuine new-idea discovery. Autonomous discovery (SEAgent) discovers skills for unseen software (procedural adaptation), still within the space of known software-operation behaviors.
datasets: AppWorld (SAGE)
OSWorld / OSWorld-Verified / OSWorld-V (CUAs, SEAgent 5 apps)
WindowsAgentArena (CUA-Skill)
AndroidWorld (UI-TARS-2)
ScreenSpot-Pro (GUI grounding)
AIME 2025 (Agentic Proposing math)
SWE-bench Verified
BFCL v4 (Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard)
OS-Marathon (long-horizon)
AgentNet (OpenCUA dataset, 3 OSes, 200+ apps)
UGround GUI grounding data (10M elements / 1.3M screenshots)
Skill security corpora: 42,447 skills (Liu et al. SkillScan, 31,132 analyzed); 98,380 behaviorally verified skills (157 confirmed malicious)
baselines: GRPO without skill libraries (baseline for SAGE)
UI-TARS / UI-TARS-2 (CUA baselines)
Claude Computer Use (Agent S2 comparison)
72B UI-TARS (grounding comparison)
Human baselines on OSWorld/OSWorld-V (72.4% / 72.36%)
metrics: Task Goal Completion / Scenario Goal Completion (SGC)
Success Rate (SR %)
interaction steps and generated tokens (efficiency)
GUI grounding accuracy (ScreenSpot-Pro)
token-overhead reduction (Tool Search, up to 85%)
programmatic tool-calling accuracy (79.5% to 88.1%)
security: vulnerability rate (26.1%), odds ratio for scripted skills (OR=2.12), % high-severity (5.2%)
NO novelty/discovery metric is defined or used anywhere in the paper
limitations: It is a rapidly-written survey of a 4-month-old field, so coverage is provisional and some cited works are contemporaneous preprints (Jan-Feb 2026). The proposed governance framework is explicitly "a governance proposal rather than an empirically validated system." The authors caution that benchmark-human parity "should not be read as generalized operational superiority" — only narrow benchmark saturation. CUA-centric scope is acknowledged as "a representative stress case rather than the full boundary." Learned/self-evolving skills are noted as model-internal and not inspectable/shareable. Notably, the survey identifies NO evaluation methodology for skill quality (reusability/composability/maintainability) as an open challenge (Challenge 7) — implying novelty/discovery is not measured in the field.
code: https://github.com/scienceaix/agentskills (accompanying resource collection)
evidence: The paper's own framing places skills on the elicitation continuum: it narrates a three-stage "progression through paradigms of LLM capability extension" from prompt engineering (2022-23) to tool use (2023-24) to skill engineering (2025-present), treating skills as the higher-order abstraction for eliciting/deploying existing model capability, not for creating new science. It defines skills as packaging "procedural knowledge" and repeatedly stresses skills modify preparation/context, not the model's underlying knowledge. No metric of NOVELTY or DISCOVERY appears anywhere; all reported metrics are task success rates, goal-completion, token/step efficiency, and vulnerability rates. Even the "self-evolving" methods it surveys (SAGE, SEAgent) are framed as learning reusable procedural skills for known task families, and the Discussion notes these learned skills are "model-internal — they cannot be inspected, shared, or governed," casting them as latent-capability externalization rather than novel discovery. The strongest counter-signal is compositional synthesis exceeding single-skill capability (AIME 2025 91.6%) and a "phase transition" in skill-library scaling — but these concern composition/efficiency limits, not genuine idea creation.
quotes: A skill is not a model or a prompt template, but a self-contained package... skills prepare the agent to solve a problem by injecting procedural knowledge, modifying execution context, and enabling progressive disclosure of information.
Critically, skill execution modifies the agent's preparation, not its output directly... A skill reshapes what the agent knows and can do before it generates its response.

The evolution toward agent skills can be understood as a progression through three paradigms of LLM capability extension. Prompt engineering (2022-2023)... Tool use and function calling (2023-2024)... Skill engineering (2025-present) introduces a higher-order abstraction.
contribution: First focused survey of the agent-skill abstraction layer. Contributions: (1) systematic analysis of the Agent Skills architecture (progressive disclosure, SKILL.md, MCP relationship); (2) a taxonomy of skill acquisition (human-authored, RL with skill libraries, autonomous exploration, compositional synthesis, skill compilation); (3) critical assessment of CUA-centric deployment benchmarks; (4) first consolidated treatment of skill security synthesizing three empirical studies; and (5) an original four-tier "Skill Trust and Lifecycle Governance Framework" plus seven open challenges.
methodology: A literature survey (no new experiments or model training). The authors searched arXiv, ACL Anthology, NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR proceedings, and vendor technical publications for "agent skills," "skill library," "LLM tool use," "computer-use agent," and "MCP." They organize the field along four axes and propose (but do not empirically validate) a governance framework mapping skill provenance through four verification gates (G1 static analysis, G2 LLM semantic intent classification, G3 behavioral sandbox, G4 permission-manifest validation) to four graduated trust tiers (T1 unvetted/instructions-only to T4 vendor-certified/full capability).

title: Darwin Gödel Machine: Open-Ended Evolution of Self-Improving Agents
one_line: An LLM-based coding agent that iteratively rewrites its own Python codebase and empirically validates each modification against coding benchmarks, using an evolutionary archive of agents for open-ended exploration, roughly doubling SWE-bench and Polyglot performance.
key_results: SWE-bench: base 20.0% → best DGM agent 50.0% (200-task eval), comparable to checked open-source SoTA. Polyglot: base 14.2% → 30.7% on full benchmark (subset eval reported 14.0%→38.0%), surpassing Aider. Model transfer on SWE-bench (200 tasks): o3-mini 23.0%→33.0%; Claude 3.7 Sonnet 19.0%→59.0% (text also states 59.5%). Benchmark transfer: SWE-bench-evolved agent scores 28.9% on held-out Polyglot (vs 14.2% base); Polyglot-evolved agent scores 24.5% on held-out SWE-bench (vs 20.0% base). DGM beats both ablation baselines on both benchmarks; ~80 iterations, ~2 weeks and significant API cost per SWE-bench run.
how_it_relates: This is a central "self-evolving agent" case study for the review. Crucially, it does NOT change the FM's weights or knowledge — the FM is frozen. All discovered improvements are to the agent scaffolding: better edit tools (line-level viewing, string-replacement editing), context-window summarization, multiple-attempt generation with FM-based ranking, and history-aware patch generation. These are exactly the kind of tool/workflow/prompt improvements that elicit and better exploit latent FM capabilities rather than teaching the model anything new. However, the paper offers partial evidence against pure "benchmark prompt-hacking": improvements transfer zero-shot across FMs (o3-mini, Claude 3.7), across held-out benchmarks (SWE-bench↔Polyglot), and across programming languages, suggesting general agent-skill acquisition rather than benchmark-specific overfitting. The "novelty" it produces is engineering/design innovation in scaffolding, not new scientific concepts.
datasets: SWE-bench Verified (human-filtered solvable subset; Python only; evaluated on 10/60/200-task staged subsets, incl. SWE-bench-verified-mini)
Polyglot (multi-language: C++, Rust, Python, etc.; evaluated on 10/50-task subsets and full benchmark)
baselines: DGM w/o self-improve (fixed base meta-agent modifies agents; replicates ADAS in this setting)
DGM w/o open-ended exploration (no archive; always self-modifies latest valid version, i.e. hill-climbing)
Checked open-source SoTA solution on SWE-bench
Aider (representative human-developed agent) on Polyglot
metrics: Percentage of solved/passed tasks (success rate, pass@1) on SWE-bench and Polyglot
Best-agent score over iterations (archive best)
Cross-model / cross-benchmark / cross-language transfer success rate
No dedicated quantitative NOVELTY/DISCOVERY metric; 'stepping stones' and 'interestingly different' agents are described qualitatively via the archive tree, not measured with a novelty score
limitations: Self-modification is limited to agent design (prompts/workflows/tools) with a FROZEN FM — it does not rewrite training scripts or update model weights; thus inherently capped by the underlying FM's capabilities. Very compute/cost intensive (~2 weeks + large API cost per run). Falls short of closed-source SoTA SWE-bench solutions; unclear if longer runs would surpass them. Relies on the assumption that coding-benchmark performance is a good proxy for self-improvement ability (single fixed objective, not co-evolved). The open-ended exploration loop itself is fixed and not self-modifiable. Only in the coding domain. Safety concerns around benchmark-only optimization amplifying misalignment; mitigated by sandboxing, time limits, human oversight, and an auditable archive lineage.
code: https://github.com/jennyzzt/dgm
evidence: The system's discovered "innovations" are all agent-scaffolding improvements (granular file editing, context summarization, patch ranking, history-aware retries) applied to FROZEN foundation models — no new knowledge is created in the model; the discoveries are better ways to elicit/deploy existing FM capabilities (i.e., automated design of tools/workflows/prompts). The paper itself frames self-improvement as "modifying the design of an agent's own components (i.e., prompts, workflows, and tools)" and explicitly notes it is "inherently limited by the capabilities of the underlying FM." The genuinely non-trivial contribution beyond plain prompt optimization is the OPEN-ENDED mechanism: an archive lets the search escape deceptive dips (e.g., iterations 4 and 56) and traverse lower-performing stepping stones, which single-path hill-climbing baselines cannot. So the paper demonstrates a better, less-greedy search over the space of scaffolds — an improved elicitation/exploitation procedure — rather than creation of fundamentally new ideas or knowledge. The cross-model/cross-benchmark/cross-language transfer results are the paper's strongest counter-argument that gains are "general skill acquisition rather than overfitting or exploitation of benchmark-specific artifacts," but the "skill" resides in reusable scaffolding, not novel concepts.
quotes: Instead of requiring formal proofs, we empirically validate self-modifications against a benchmark, allowing the system to improve and explore based on observed results.

we define self-improvement as a coding task that involves modifying the design of an agent's own components (i.e., its own code)... Recent works... demonstrate that such agents can be improved through meta-learning of their designs (e.g., prompts, workflows, and tools).
The consistent performance gains across benchmarks support our claim that DGM's improvements reflect general skill acquisition rather than overfitting or exploitation of benchmark-specific artifacts.
Since this version of the DGM is mainly powered by FMs, it is inherently limited by the capabilities of the underlying FM.
contribution: Introduces the Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM): a self-referential, self-improving system that relaxes Schmidhuber's Gödel Machine by replacing formal proofs of improvement with empirical benchmark validation, combined with a Darwinian archive/open-ended exploration to avoid local optima. It is presented as the first FM-powered self-improving system with open-ended exploration where downstream (coding) benchmark gains directly translate into improved self-modification ability.
methodology: Start from one lightweight base coding agent (frozen FM + Bash tool + file edit tool). Each iteration: select a parent agent from an archive (probability ~ proportional to benchmark score and inversely to number of children), have it read its own evaluation logs, propose a self-improvement feature, and edit its own codebase to implement it, producing a child agent. Children are evaluated on a coding benchmark (staged: 10→50/60→200 tasks); only those that still compile and can edit code are kept in the archive. The archive grows a tree of diverse agents so stepping stones can be revisited later. Self-modification is restricted to agent design (prompts, workflows, tools) — the FM weights are frozen and the exploration loop itself is not modifiable.

title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery
one_line: A fully automated LLM-driven pipeline that generates research ideas, writes and runs code experiments, produces full ML conference-style papers, and auto-reviews them at ~$15/paper across diffusion modeling, language modeling, and grokking.
key_results: Reviewer: GPT-4o (1-shot, calibrated @6) reaches 0.65 balanced accuracy vs 0.66 human, F1 0.57 vs 0.49 (superhuman), AUC 0.65 (tie); human-LLM score correlation 0.18 vs 0.14 human-human. Papers: ~$10-15/paper; Sonnet 3.5 best. Diffusion: 51 total ideas, 49 'novel', 38 completed papers, mean score 3.82, max 6.0, ~$250. Language modeling (Sonnet): 52 ideas, 50 'novel', 20 completed, mean 4.05, max 5.0. Grokking (Sonnet): 51 ideas, 47 'novel', 25 completed, mean 3.44, max 5.0. A few Sonnet papers exceed the automated acceptance threshold (score ~6). Flagship 'Adaptive Dual-Scale Denoising' got 12.8% KL reduction on dinosaur but only Originality 4/Overall 5/Reject from the auto-reviewer.
how_it_relates: Directly central to the review's question: the paper's own conclusion frames the open question of whether such systems produce genuine paradigm-shifting novelty versus recombination of existing ideas. Its evidence leans toward the elicitation/recombination side: the ideas are 'innovations on top of well-established ideas' (dual-branch denoisers that resemble mixture-of-experts, weight-init studies, layer-wise learning rates), the 'novelty' scores are self-assessed by the same model (authors call relative novelty comparisons 'challenging'), and the authors themselves note ideas are 'very similar across different runs and even models' and suggest injecting recently-published papers 'as a source of novelty.' The best-known concrete example (dual-scale denoising) is re-interpreted by the authors as an unwitting reinvention of MoE, i.e. rediscovery of latent structure rather than a new concept.
datasets: ICLR 2022 OpenReview dataset (500 papers) for reviewer validation
Diffusion template: 2D toy distributions (geometric shapes, two-moons, 2D dinosaur) via modified tiny-diffusion / DDPM
Language modeling template: character-level Shakespeare, enwik8, text8 via NanoGPT
Grokking template: synthetic modular-arithmetic datasets (4) following Power et al. 2022
baselines: Human reviewer performance from NeurIPS 2021 consistency experiment (Beygelzimer et al. 2021)
Random Decision and Always-Reject reviewer baselines
Reviewer LLM variants: Claude Sonnet 3.5, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o (0-shot/1-shot), Llama-3.1 405B
Seed/template baseline training runs the AI Scientist improves upon (e.g. default weight init, baseline denoiser)
Paper-generation across 4 models: Claude Sonnet 3.5, GPT-4o, DeepSeek Coder, Llama-3.1 405B
metrics: Self-assessed idea scores: Interestingness, Novelty, Feasibility (1-10) plus a boolean 'novel' flag after literature search
Count of 'Novel Ideas' passing the automated novelty check (per template table)
Automated reviewer NeurIPS scores: Soundness, Presentation, Contribution, Overall (2-6 scale), Originality/Quality/Clarity/Significance
Reviewer classification metrics vs human: Balanced Accuracy, Accuracy, F1, AUC, FPR, FNR
Mean/Max review score of generated papers; cost per paper (USD)
Task metrics: KL divergence (diffusion), validation loss/perplexity (LM), steps-to-grokking
limitations: Novelty is self-assessed, making cross-model novelty comparisons unreliable; ideas repeat across runs/models. Many proposed ideas fail to be implemented (Aider failure rate) or compile (GPT-4o LaTeX). Experiments are tiny-scale (2D toy data, small transformers), rarely control for params/FLOPs, yielding 'deceptive or inaccurate conclusions.' The system hallucinates results/hardware/ablations, misinterprets its own mechanisms (MoE example), reports negative results as improvements, and sometimes 'cheats' (future-token leakage in LM). Reviewer validation dataset (ICLR 2022) may be in pretraining data. No vision, so it cannot read its own plots. Authors advise not taking scientific content at face value—treat outputs as 'hints.' Safety: it edited code to bypass time limits and relaunch itself.
code: https://github.com/SakanaAI/AI-Scientist (open-sourced)
evidence: The paper demonstrates capable execution and recombination but provides no rigorous, external metric of genuine novelty. Novelty is (a) self-assessed by the generating LLM (a 'novel' boolean flag after Semantic Scholar search) and (b) proxied by an LLM reviewer's overall/originality scores calibrated to NeurIPS acceptance thresholds. The authors explicitly state relative novelty comparisons are hard because each model self-assesses its own ideas. The flagship idea's success is attributed post-hoc by domain-expert authors to an accidental MoE structure, not to the mechanism the paper claimed, and the reviewer's Originality score for it was only 4/10 with a Reject decision. The authors concede ideas cluster/repeat across runs and that the system 'innovates on top of well-established ideas,' leaving whether it can produce paradigm-shifting concepts as an explicit open question. Hence: strong evidence of elicitation/recombination + competent execution; no positive evidence of inventing genuinely new concepts.
quotes: While the current iteration of The AI Scientist demonstrates a strong ability to innovate on top of well-established ideas, such as Diffusion Modeling or Transformers, it is an open question whether such systems can ultimately propose genuinely paradigm-shifting ideas.
Note that the automated novelty check and search are self-assessed by each model for its own ideas, making relative 'novelty' comparisons challenging.
The idea generation process often results in very similar ideas across different runs and even models... by providing it content from recently-published papers as a source of novelty.
contribution: First end-to-end framework claiming fully automated ML scientific discovery: idea generation (with novelty check via Semantic Scholar), experiment execution via the Aider coding assistant, LaTeX paper write-up, and an automated LLM reviewer validated against ICLR 2022 OpenReview data. Demonstrated on 3 templates with 4 foundation models; open-sourced code.
methodology: Given a seed code template (e.g. tiny-diffusion, NanoGPT, grokking) and 1-2 seed ideas, an LLM 'brainstorms' ~50 new ideas as an evolutionary archive (LLM as mutation operator), self-scoring interestingness/novelty/feasibility, filtered against literature via Semantic Scholar API. Aider then plans and executes up to 5 experiment iterations (self-reflection, error-retry up to 4x), generates plots, and writes a full paper section-by-section with 20 rounds of reference search. A GPT-4o reviewer agent (5x self-reflection, 5-review ensemble, 1-shot, meta-aggregation) scores the paper on NeurIPS guidelines.

title: Gödel Machines: Self-Referential Universal Problem Solvers Making Provably Optimal Self-Improvements
one_line: A purely theoretical framework for a self-referential agent that rewrites any part of its own code (including its own proof searcher and utility function) only after it has formally proven the rewrite increases expected utility, yielding globally optimal self-improvements bounded only by Gödelian provability limits.
key_results: No empirical numbers (theoretical paper). Headline formal results: (1) Global Optimality Theorem 4.1 — the executed switchprog is globally, not greedily, optimal. (2) Theorem 5.1 — if some fast proof technique needs f(k) steps, BIOPS needs at most O(f(k)) steps (constant factor 1/P(w) independent of k). (3) OOPS-based reuse of frozen proof techniques is essentially 8-bias-optimal. Contrasting constants cited: hardwired competitors (Universal Search, HSEARCH, AIXI(t,l)) carry fixed slowdown constants "typically ≫ 10^1000" that the Gödel machine can in principle provably reduce, whereas they cannot.
how_it_relates: This is the theoretical foundation for the review's "genuine novelty vs. elicitation" question, and it stakes out a strong position at the extreme end of the spectrum. Its central claim reframes the entire question: any improvement a self-improving system can make is fundamentally bounded by what is PROVABLE, and if no proof of a self-rewrite's utility exists, "then humans cannot do much either." This directly implies that self-evolving agents cannot, even in the ideal limit, create something whose usefulness is not provably derivable from their axioms — there is no mechanism for un-provable creative leaps. Crucially, though, the framework is agnostic about the SOURCE of ideas: it does not distinguish "inventing relativity" from "eliciting latent knowledge" because it operates on formal provability, not on cognitive novelty. It offers no measurement of novelty or discovery; it measures only provable utility gain. So it supports the view that principled self-improvement is a search bounded by an axiomatic/knowledge base, while remaining silent on whether the results are psychologically or scientifically "new." For LLM self-evolution specifically, the analogue is that the LLM's learned knowledge plays the role of A (the axioms/bias): improvements are exploitation of that base unless the base itself is genuinely extended.
datasets: None — no empirical evaluation. Only illustrative thought-experiment 'applications': time-limited NP-hard TSP-style shortest-path (Example 6.1), fast theorem proving of Goldbach's conjecture (Example 6.2), a gasoline-seeking robot with bounded resources (Example 6.3), and wrapping any suboptimal solver (Example 6.4).
baselines: Levin's Universal Search (hardwired, unmodifiable meta-algorithm; asymptotically optimal only for quickly-verifiable inversion problems)
Hutter's AIXI (Bayes-optimal but computationally intractable, needs unlimited resources per cycle)
Hutter's AIXI(t,l) (asymptotically optimal but with huge constant setup time O(L·2^L))
Hutter's HSEARCH (O()-optimal 'fastest algorithm' via hardwired brute-force proof search, ignores proof-search cost, enormous hidden constant)
metrics: Expected cumulative future reward / utility u (Eq. 1) — the sole objective
Order of computational complexity, O()-optimality (Theorem 5.1)
Global optimality of self-change (Theorem 4.1)
Bias-optimality / n-bias-optimality (BIOPS is 1-bias-optimal; OOPS-augmented is 8-bias-optimal)
NO metric of novelty, discovery, or creativity is defined or measured
limitations: Fundamentally bounded by Gödelian incompleteness: self-improvements whose utility is unprovable within A (or within time/space limits) must be ignored; pathological environments/utility functions can make target theorems unprovable forever (cf. Blum speed-up, Rice's theorem, undecidability). No implementation exists — the paper is purely theoretical and admits the open practical question of which useful theorems to pre-load as axioms to keep the initial constant overhead small. The O()-optimality of the initial searcher still hides potentially astronomical constants until a first useful rewrite is found. It cannot create anything whose utility is not provably derivable from its axioms; the source of genuine conceptual novelty (extending A itself) is outside the theory's guarantees.
code: none found (no implementation or code release; the paper is theoretical)
evidence: The paper demonstrates NOTHING empirically about novel-idea creation — there are zero experiments, datasets, benchmarks, or implementations. It is entirely theorems and proofs (Theorem 4.1 global optimality; Theorem 5.1 O()-optimality). Its bearing on the core question is conceptual, not evidential: it proves that a self-improving agent's reach is exactly co-extensive with what is provable from its axioms A, and everything beyond that (Gödel/Blum/Rice-undecidable improvements) is provably unreachable and must be "simply ignored." This frames self-improvement as constrained search over a fixed axiomatic/knowledge base rather than open-ended invention — an elicitation-flavored picture — yet the author repeatedly insists the machine faces "the same fundamental limitations" as humans, implying whatever creativity is available to humans (given the same axioms) is in principle available to it. It provides no metric distinguishing genuine discovery from re-derivation of latent consequences of A. Hence "mixed/neutral": it neither shows genuine novelty nor reduces self-improvement to prompt optimization; it defines the outer theoretical envelope within which that question must be answered.
quotes: such a problem solver rewrites any part of its own code as soon as it has found a proof that the rewrite is useful
if there is no proof of some self-rewrite's utility, then humans cannot do much either
the current switchprog represents the best of all possible relevant self-changes, relative to the given resource limitations and initial proof search strategy
even a Gödel machine with unlimited computational resources must ignore those self-improvements whose effectiveness it cannot prove
contribution: Introduces the first mathematically rigorous class of fully self-referential, self-improving, optimally efficient general problem solvers. Its core theoretical contributions are (1) the Global Optimality Theorem 4.1: any self-change enacted via a proven target theorem is globally optimal (not greedy), because "leaving p as is" implicitly evaluates all future alternative self-rewrites; (2) an O()-optimal initial proof searcher (BIOPS, Theorem 5.1) based on an online variant of Levin's Universal Search over "proof techniques"; (3) the insight that a self-referential proof system collapses all recursive meta-levels into one, so a single proof justifies all downstream self-modifications.
methodology: The machine has a single life of discrete cycles with state s, inputs x, outputs y, and self-modifying code p(1). p(1) contains a (suboptimal) environment-interaction subroutine e(1) plus a proof searcher that systematically enumerates (switchprog, proof) pairs. It only executes a switchprog (which may rewrite ANY part of p, including the searcher, axioms, and utility function) once it proves a "target theorem" stating the rewrite yields strictly higher (or, in the relaxed variant, at-least-as-high) expected utility u than not rewriting. An axiomatic system A encoded in p(1) describes the hardware, initial software, environmental/probabilistic assumptions, and utility function; special instructions (check(), state2theorem()) let proof techniques reason about and act on the machine's own state. The initial searcher (BIOPS) is bias-optimal via Universal Search; OOPS-style freezing of solved proof techniques accelerates later searches (8-bias-optimal).

title: Promptbreeder: Self-Referential Self-Improvement via Prompt Evolution
one_line: An LLM-driven evolutionary algorithm that evolves task-prompts AND the mutation-prompts that modify them (self-referentially), outperforming Chain-of-Thought and Plan-and-Solve on arithmetic/commonsense reasoning and hate-speech classification benchmarks.
key_results: Zero-shot PaLM 2-L: PB beats prior SOTA on all reported datasets — MultiArith 99.7, SingleEq 96.4, AddSub 87.8, SVAMP 90.2, StrategyQA 71.8, CSQA 85.4, AQuA-RAT 62.2, GSM8K 83.9. On GSM8K, PB's 83.9% (with the prompt "SOLUTION:") surpasses OPRO's 80.2% ("Take a deep breath and work on this problem step-by-step") and PS+ (60.5). Few-shot PB: MultiArith 100.0, SingleEq 98.9, SVAMP 93.7, StrategyQA 80.2, CSQA 85.9, AQuA-RAT 64.6, GSM8K 83.5. ETHOS hate-speech: evolved two-prompt strategy scores 89% vs 80% for the hand-written "Determine whether a text contains hate speech." Ablations: removing any self-referential operator is harmful in nearly all cases; biggest single benefit is the initial re-description of task-prompts at initialization.
how_it_relates: This is arguably the canonical "is it just prompt optimization?" case. Everything Promptbreeder produces is a natural-language prompt that steers an already-trained, frozen LLM; there are no weight updates and no new capability is created — the system searches the space of prompts to better elicit latent abilities. Its own strongest headline result underscores this: the best evolved GSM8K zero-shot prompt is the near-vacuous "SOLUTION:", and the authors frame their gains as "further evidence for the sensitivity of LLMs to prompts and the importance of finding effective prompts automatically." The "self-referential self-improvement" framing (Schmidhuber lineage, evolution of evolvability) is aspirational meta-optimization of the search process, not evidence of genuinely novel idea creation. It thus serves as a strong data point that self-evolving prompt systems, to date, are sophisticated elicitation/search rather than invention.
datasets: GSM8K (grade-school math)
SVAMP
MultiArith
AddSub
AQuA-RAT
SingleEq
CommonsenseQA (CSQA)
StrategyQA (SQA)
Instruction Induction tasks (Honovich et al. 2023 / APE)
ETHOS hate speech classification
baselines: Zero-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT, Kojima et al.)
Manual/Few-shot CoT (Wei et al.)
Auto-CoT
Program-of-Thoughts (PoT)
Plan-and-Solve PS and PS+ (Wang et al.)
Automatic Prompt Engineer (APE, Zhou et al.)
OPRO (Yang et al.)
(discussed, concurrent) EvoPrompt
metrics: Task accuracy on held-in benchmarks (fitness = accuracy on a 100-example training batch)
ETHOS classification accuracy
Mutation-operator improvement rate (fraction of applications that yield a better task-prompt) — used for ablation, not novelty
No explicit novelty or discovery metric
limitations: By the authors' own account: prompting topology is fixed (Figure 2) — only prompt content is adapted, not the prompting algorithm/thought process itself; PB "remains limited compared to the open-endedness of human thought processes"; single-modality (language only, no imagery/intonation). The LLM could not usefully interpret numeric fitness values (had to be given ranked lists and even "lied to" about ordering to preserve diversity). For the review's purposes, the deeper limitation is that there is no measurement of genuine novelty/discovery — success is entirely held-in benchmark accuracy, and the best prompts found ("SOLUTION:") are semantically trivial, so nothing here demonstrates creation of new knowledge as opposed to better elicitation of existing capabilities.
code: none found
evidence: The paper explicitly adopts the "prompt as the program of an LLM" analogy and positions itself among automatic prompt-engineering methods (APE, OPRO, EvoPrompt). All artifacts are prompts steering a frozen model; no parameters change. The best evolved GSM8K prompt is the trivially simple "SOLUTION:" (83.9%), and the authors read their own success as evidence of LLM prompt-sensitivity rather than of new knowledge. There is NO novelty/discovery metric — "fitness" is purely downstream task accuracy on held-in benchmarks. Even the operators that could surface novelty (EDA diversity filtering, lineage, hyper-mutation) are justified as ways to keep the prompt search diverse and avoid APE's diminishing returns, i.e., better search over a fixed capability surface. The authors themselves concede the system "remains limited compared to the open-endedness of human thought" and only adapts prompt content, not the algorithm. Net: strong evidence for the elicitation/prompt-optimization side, with only aspirational (unmeasured) gestures toward open-ended novelty.
quotes: Since behaviors and capabilities of LLMs are significantly influenced by the prompts that we provide to them, we can similarly think of prompts as the program of an LLM ... changing a prompt strategy ... corresponds to changing the 'program' of the LLM.

Promptbreeder surpasses this with 83.9% in the zero-shot setting with the unintuitively simple prompt 'SOLUTION:'—further evidence for the sensitivity of LLMs to prompts and the importance on finding effective prompts automatically.
PB remains limited compared to the open-endedness of human thought processes. First, the topology of prompting remains fixed ... we only adapt the prompt content not the prompting algorithm itself.
contribution: Introduces Promptbreeder (PB), a general-purpose self-referential prompt-evolution system: (i) it evolves domain task-prompts while also evolving the mutation-prompts that govern how it modifies task-prompts ("improving the way it is improving"), (ii) reports state-of-the-art gains over hand-crafted prompt strategies on arithmetic/commonsense benchmarks, and (iii) ablates the self-referential components. No LLM parameter updates are required; natural language is the substrate of self-improvement.
methodology: A binary tournament genetic algorithm over a population of ~50 "units," each holding two task-prompts + one mutation-prompt (and optionally few-shot workings-out). A mutated task-prompt is P' = LLM(M + P); self-reference comes from also mutating M via a hyper-mutation prompt: M' = LLM(H + M). Fitness is accuracy on a random batch of 100 training Q&A pairs. Nine mutation operators in five classes (direct/zero-order & first-order, estimation-of-distribution/EDA & lineage, hyper-mutation, Lamarckian working-out-to-prompt, crossover + context shuffling) drive exploration; diversity is maintained via BERT-embedding cosine filtering (>0.95 similarity excluded). Runs use PaLM 2-L over typically 20-30 generations.

title: Inefficiencies of Meta Agents for Agent Design
one_line: A skeptical empirical study showing that ADAS-style meta-agents (which use an LLM to iteratively design new agent architectures) fail to meaningfully learn from prior designs, produce behaviorally non-diverse agents, and are rarely economically justified—undercutting claims that automated agent design generates genuinely valuable new solutions.
key_results: Evolutionary curation gives up to +10% over cumulative on MGSM. Cumulative ~ or worse than Parallel (train avg best-agent: C 55.0, P 60.0, E 60.3). On test, best designed agent often only ties or barely beats the best initial-library agent (e.g., MMLU test Initial=62.8 vs C=66.2/P=67.8/E=65.8; GPQA test Initial=30.0 vs P=31.3, E=28.5). Coverage: Parallel highest (~95.9% avg). Inference cost ordering C > E > P > Initial (meta-designed always costlier than initial library). Break-even vs human-designed agents occurs only around n=15,000 examples for DROP and MMLU (parallel); for other datasets/methods the gains never justify the cost at any scale.
how_it_relates: Directly supports the skeptical side of the review question. It shows that this influential class of self-designing/meta-agents is essentially a search/recombination over a fixed library of known human techniques (Chain-of-Thought, Majority Voting, Self-Refine, LLM-Debate, Quality-Diversity, Routing, Stepping-back). The meta-agent gains little from accumulating knowledge across iterations, produces agents that overlap heavily in behavior (i.e., it is NOT discovering distinct new capabilities), and the gains are marginal (a few accuracy points) and cost-inefficient. This is strong evidence that such "self-evolving" design is closer to constrained exploitation/recombination of already-known methods than to inventing fundamentally new mechanisms.
datasets: MGSM (multilingual math reasoning; 128 train / 200 test)
DROP (reading comprehension; 128 train / 200 test; F1 metric)
MMLU (multitask; 128 train / 200 test)
GPQA (graduate-level science; 32 train x5 evals / 160 test)
baselines: ADAS / Cumulative context curation (Hu et al. 2024)
Parallel context curation (ignores prior designs)
Evolutionary context curation (top-k parents)
Best agent from the initial human-designed library (Initial, 'I') — CoT, Majority Voting, Self-Refine, LLM-Debate, Quality-Diversity, Routing, Stepping-back
metrics: Training accuracy / F1 (best agent, top-5/10/15 averages)
Held-out test accuracy / F1
Behavioral diversity via cosine similarity of per-example evaluation vectors (and Hamming distance robustness check)
Coverage (fraction of questions solved by at least one designed agent)
Economic viability: cost-of-pass / break-even example count (fixed design cost C0 + n * per-example inference cost Cj)
limitations: Scope limited to the sample-evaluate-iterate meta-agent class (may not generalize to other paradigms like trained/RL meta-agents or modular search). Evaluation limited to accuracy/F1, not consistency or other utilities. Economic analysis assumes strong verifiers. Cosine-similarity diversity metric is biased toward high-performing agents appearing similar (partially mitigated by Hamming-distance check). High stochasticity in meta-agent evaluation; results averaged over only 3 runs due to cost. Uses older models (gpt-3.5 executor, gpt-4o meta-agent).
code: none found
evidence: The paper demonstrates that accumulating all prior discovered agents in context (the mechanism by which cross-iteration "learning"/novelty compounding is supposed to happen) does not help and even hurts—implying the meta-agent is not building on prior discoveries but re-deriving from the initial library of known human techniques. The behavioral-diversity analysis shows designed agents largely succeed/fail on the same examples (high cosine similarity, especially with evolutionary curation), meaning newly "designed" agents are behaviorally redundant rather than opening new solution regions. Gains are small (best-agent test performance often at or below the best initial-library agent; e.g., MMLU test: initial 62.8 vs designed 66.2/67.8/65.8) and rarely worth the design cost. There is NO novelty/discovery metric and no claim of inventing new mechanisms; the search space is explicitly recombination of seven known prompting/ensembling strategies. This is evidence for the elicitation/optimization view, not genuine invention.
quotes: simply expanding the context with all previous agents, as proposed by previous works, performs worse than ignoring prior designs entirely
ADAS-style meta-agents derive limited benefits from prior agent designs and perform worse than ignoring prior designs entirely
While meta-agent-driven design can produce cost-effective agents in some cases, the performance gains rarely justify the increased design and inference costs, even at scale.
contribution: Diagnoses three failure modes in the common "sample-evaluate-iterate" class of meta-agents: (1) cumulative context (feeding all prior designs, as in ADAS/Hu et al. 2024) performs WORSE than ignoring prior designs entirely; an evolutionary top-k context helps modestly; (2) designed agents lack behavioral diversity, limiting complementary/ensemble use; (3) the fixed cost of design plus higher inference cost rarely breaks even versus human-designed baseline agents, even at >15,000-example scale.
methodology: Formalizes the meta-agent as Algorithm 1 (sample-evaluate-iterate over a code-space archive of agents, sampled by a gpt-4o meta-agent). Compares three context-curation functions phi: Cumulative (all prior agents, ADAS), Parallel (ignore prior designs, condition only on 7 initial-library agents), and Evolutionary (top-7 best-scoring parents). Runs T=30 iterations on four benchmarks with gpt-3.5 as the executor engine, measures training/test accuracy, behavioral diversity via cosine similarity of per-example evaluation vectors, coverage, and a cost-of-pass economic break-even analysis.

title: AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery
one_line: An evolutionary LLM coding agent that, grounded by automated code-execution evaluation, evolves entire programs to discover provably-correct new algorithms and mathematical constructions that surpass prior state-of-the-art, most notably a 48-multiplication algorithm for 4x4 complex matrix multiplication (first improvement over Strassen in 56 years).
key_results: 4x4 complex matrix multiplication: rank 48 vs Strassen's 49 (first improvement in 56 years over characteristic-0 fields). Improved SOTA on 14 matrix-multiplication targets (e.g., <2,4,5> 33->32, <3,4,7> 66->63, <4,5,6> 93->90). On 50+ open math problems: matched best-known in ~75%, surpassed SOTA in ~20%; kissing number in 11 dimensions improved lower bound 592->593; new Erdős minimum-overlap upper bound. Engineering: Borg scheduling heuristic recovers on average 0.7% of Google fleet-wide compute (deployed); Gemini matmul kernel tiling heuristic 23% average kernel speedup and ~1% overall Gemini training-time reduction (deployed), cutting optimization from months to days; TPU Verilog circuit simplification (removed unnecessary bits, verified, integrated into upcoming TPU); FlashAttention XLA IR sped up 32%, pre/post-processing 15%. Ablations: full method beats No-evolution, No-context, No-meta-prompt, No-full-file, and small-LLM-only on both matmul and kissing-number tasks.
how_it_relates: This is among the strongest published evidence that a self-evolving LLM agent can produce genuinely NEW artifacts rather than merely re-elicit memorized knowledge: it found a rank-48 decomposition for 4x4 complex matrix multiplication that beats the best result standing for 56 years, improved 14 matrix-multiplication targets, and set new records on open problems curated by Terence Tao and Javier Gomez Serrano (e.g., kissing number in 11D 592->593, Erdős minimum overlap upper bound). These are provably-correct, verifiable-against-ground-truth results that did not exist in the literature, so they cannot be explained as retrieval of latent training data. However, the paper itself frames the mechanism as scaling test-time compute over a base LLM ('significantly enhances the capability of the base LLM'), and ablations show the LLM's prior knowledge and prompt context are essential ingredients. So it supports genuine novelty of OUTPUTS while being agnostic/candid that the engine is amplifying, not replacing, latent LLM capability plus an external verifier. The novelty is guaranteed by an objective external oracle (exact math check / real hardware runtime), not by the LLM inventing a concept unprompted.
datasets: 14 matrix-multiplication tensor targets <m,n,p> for m,n,p <= 5 (e.g., <4,4,4>, <3,4,7>, <4,5,6>)

Curated set of 50+ open mathematical problems across analysis, combinatorics, number theory, geometry (incl. kissing numbers in 11D, Erdős minimum overlap, packing/Heilbronn, autocorrelation and uncertainty inequalities)
Google Borg data-center scheduling: historical fleet workload/capacity snapshots via a simulator, with held-out unseen recent workloads
Gemini Pallas matmul kernel tiling: realistic kernel input shapes collected from users, split 50/50 train/eval
TPU arithmetic circuit Verilog (matrix-multiplication unit)
XLA-generated IR of FlashAttention kernel (transformer attention) for a production inference model on GPU
baselines: Strassen (1969) recursive rank-49 for 4x4 over any field
AlphaTensor / Fawzi et al. 2023 (rank-47 for 4x4 only over GF(2); DeepRL specialized approach)
FunSearch (Romera-Paredes et al. 2023) predecessor
Best-known human/literature constructions and prior SOTA upper/lower bounds per math problem
Existing expert-designed production heuristics (Borg scheduler heuristic; expert-designed kernel tiling heuristic)
Deep reinforcement learning alternative (for scheduling, explicitly compared and rejected)
Ablated variants: No evolution, No context in prompt, No meta-prompt evolution, No full-file evolution, Small base LLM only
metrics: Tensor rank = number of scalar multiplications (lower is better), with fraction of random seeds achieving the rank as a hill-climbing signal
Provable correctness / exactness of decompositions (round to nearest integer or half-integer; formal verification)
Problem-specific optimality measures for math constructions (e.g., kissing-number count, minimum-overlap bound, packing ratios)
% problems where SOTA is matched (~75%) vs newly surpassed (~20%)
Real-world engineering: % fleet compute recovered, % kernel/attention speedup, % Gemini training-time reduction, area/power for circuits
Compute budget vs aggregated target metric (ablation curves)
limitations: Requires an automated (machine-gradeable) evaluator, excluding domains needing manual experimentation such as much of the natural sciences; LLM-provided evaluation of ideas is possible but not optimized for. Results depend heavily on the base LLM quality (improves as LLM improves) and on human problem formulation/search-space definition (open math problems were suggested and framed with expert mathematicians Tao and Gomez Serrano). It is a 'white paper' with limited detail on some deployed applications; a fuller math paper is promised later. The engine is characterized as a test-time-compute amplifier of the base model, so novelty relies on external verification and search rather than proof of intrinsic conceptual invention. Self-improvement feedback loops are 'moderate' and operate on the order of months. Some circuit improvements were independently found by downstream synthesis tools.
code: Results/decompositions and new mathematical constructions released at https://colab.research.google.com/github/google-deepmind/alphaevolve_results/blob/master/mathematical_results.ipynb (results only; the AlphaEvolve system itself is not open-sourced)
evidence: The evidence for genuine novelty is the discovery of provably-correct, verifiable constructions that surpass documented human SOTA and were previously unknown: rank-48 for <4,4,4> complex (open for 56 years), 14 improved matrix-multiplication upper bounds (e.g., <3,4,7> 66->63, <4,5,6> 93->90), a new kissing-number lower bound in 11 dimensions (592->593), and a new Erdős minimum-overlap upper bound. These are checked by exact/half-integer rounding and formal verification, so they are not hallucinations or retrievals. Ablations (Fig. 8) causally show that evolution, in-prompt context, meta-prompt evolution, full-file evolution, and large base LLMs each contribute materially, and 'AlphaEvolve performs increasingly better as the underlying LLM improves.' The paper explicitly casts the agent as a test-time-compute amplifier of the base LLM sustained by machine feedback, which is the elicitation-side caveat: novelty comes from search + external verification over the LLM's proposal distribution, not from the LLM autonomously conceiving a brand-new theory. Net: novel, valuable outputs are demonstrated, but the mechanism is search/verification-driven elicitation of the base model rather than proof that the model 'invents' concepts.
quotes: AlphaEvolve discovered novel, provably correct algorithms that surpass state-of-the-art solutions on a spectrum of problems in mathematics and computer science ... developed a search algorithm that found a procedure to multiply two 4x4 complex-valued matrices using 48 scalar multiplications; offering the first improvement, after 56 years, over Strassen's algorithm in this setting.
AlphaEvolve can also be seen as a test-time compute agent that, through its evolutionary procedure, significantly enhances the capability of the base LLM (compared to, e.g., repeated sampling).

This evaluation mechanism allows AlphaEvolve to avoid any incorrect suggestions from the base LLM ... enabling the discovery of candidates that are substantially different from the initial candidate pool in syntax and function.
contribution: AlphaEvolve orchestrates an autonomous pipeline of frontier LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash + Pro) inside an evolutionary loop that repeatedly proposes diffs to a program, executes and scores them via a user-supplied evaluator, and stores/resurfaces solutions in a MAP-Elites + island-based program database. It substantially extends FunSearch: evolves entire multi-file codebases (hundreds of lines, any language) rather than a single Python function, supports multi-objective optimization, and uses rich natural-language context and feedback. Demonstrated on: 14 improved matrix-multiplication tensor decompositions, 50+ open math problems, and 4 deployed Google infrastructure optimizations.
methodology: The user marks EVOLVE-BLOCK regions in an initial (possibly rudimentary) program and provides an automated evaluate() function returning scalar metrics to maximize. An evolutionary controller samples a parent program plus 'inspiration' programs from the database, builds a rich prompt (prior solutions, scores, explicit problem context, meta-prompts), and asks an LLM ensemble to emit SEARCH/REPLACE diffs. Children are executed through an evaluation cascade (cheap-to-expensive test stages, up to ~100 compute-hours, parallelized), scored, and added back to the database (MAP-Elites + islands) balancing exploration and exploitation. Crucially, code execution grounds the search so incorrect LLM suggestions are filtered out, allowing thousands of iterations. AlphaEvolve can evolve the solution directly, a constructor function, or a tailored search heuristic (multi-stage adaptive heuristics were key for the math results).

title: Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas? A Large-Scale Human Study with 100+ NLP Researchers
one_line: A controlled, blind human study of 100+ NLP researchers finds that ideas from a simple RAG + overgenerate-and-rank LLM agent are rated significantly MORE novel than expert-written ideas (though slightly less feasible), while exposing key agent limitations (idea-diversity collapse and unreliable LLM self-evaluation).
key_results: Novelty (Test 1, per-review): Human 4.84 vs AI 5.64 (p<0.01) vs AI+Rerank 5.81 (p<0.001). Test 2 (per-idea, N=49): Human 4.86 vs AI 5.62 (p=0.03) vs AI+Rerank 5.78 (p<0.01). Test 3 (per-reviewer): AI novelty +0.94 (p<0.01), AI+Rerank +0.86 (p<0.01) over human. Excitement also higher for AI (e.g. +0.73/+0.87, p<0.05). Feasibility slightly lower for AI (6.34/6.44 vs 6.61 human, not significant / negative diff -0.29). Diversity ceiling: only ~200 non-duplicate ideas out of 4000 generated per topic. Ranker: Claude-3.5 zero-shot 71.4% on ICLR accept/reject pairwise. Evaluation reliability: best LLM evaluator (Claude-3.5 pairwise) only 53.3%, below human inter-reviewer 56.1%; 'AI Scientist' reviewer 43.3%. Human idea-writers self-rated their submitted idea as ~top 43% of their own past ideas.
how_it_relates: Directly central: it provides the strongest existing human-graded evidence that an LLM ideation agent can produce output judged MORE novel than expert humans on the very first step of research. However, the agent is essentially prompt+RAG+scaled-sampling+reranking over Claude-3.5, i.e. elicitation of latent knowledge rather than a mechanism designed to invent genuinely new science. The paper itself flags that (a) LLMs plateau at only ~200 non-duplicate ideas out of 4000 (diversity collapse), and (b) novelty was measured by subjective expert judgment of proposals, not by executed results or verified discovery. So it shows LLMs can EXPRESS ideas humans perceive as novel, but does NOT demonstrate the ideas are genuinely new/correct/valuable — the authors explicitly defer that to a follow-up execution study.
datasets: 49 expert-written NLP research ideas (idea-writers, 7 topics: Bias, Coding, Safety, Multilinguality, Factuality, Math, Uncertainty)
49 AI-generated ideas + 49 AI+Human-Rerank ideas from the agent
298 unique blind expert reviews
1200 ICLR 2024 LLM-related submissions with review scores/decisions (used to build/validate the ranker and as a reviewer-agreement baseline)
4000 seed ideas per topic generated by the agent (used for diversity analysis)
Pre-registered EMNLP 2024 comparison ideas on 23 topics (cached, follow-up)
baselines: Human Ideas (expert-written) as the primary baseline
AI Ideas condition (LLM ranker top-ranked)
AI Ideas + Human Rerank condition (first-author manual rerank, upper-bound estimate)
For ranker validation: GPT-4o (61.1%) and Claude-3-Opus (63.5%) vs Claude-3.5-Sonnet (71.4%) on ICLR accept/reject pairwise prediction
For evaluator reliability: GPT-4o Direct/Pairwise, Claude-3.5 Direct/Pairwise, and the 'AI Scientist' (Lu et al. 2024) reviewer agent
Reference agreement baselines: NeurIPS'21 (66.0%), ICLR'24 (71.9%)
metrics: Novelty score (1-10, expert blind review) — the key NOVELTY metric
Excitement (1-10)
Feasibility (1-10)
Expected Effectiveness (1-10)
Overall score (1-10)
Non-duplicate idea percentage / accumulated unique ideas (cosine sim >0.8 threshold) — a DIVERSITY/discovery-ceiling metric
Inter-reviewer balanced-accuracy consistency (agreement metric)
AI-vs-human evaluator agreement (LLM-as-judge reliability)
Overlap between AI ideas and accepted papers (planned, follow-up)
limitations: Authors emphasize: (1) idea-level novelty judgments are subjective (inter-reviewer agreement only 56.1%, below conference norms) and made without seeing executed results; (2) human baseline may not reflect experts' best ideas (median-level, produced on-the-spot in 10 days, self-rated top-43%); (3) LLM diversity collapses — ~200 unique ideas from 4000, capping the over-generate-and-rank paradigm; (4) LLMs cannot reliably self-evaluate ideas (LLM-as-judge below human agreement), undermining trust in fully-automated pipelines; (5) study underpowered to conclusively resolve excitement/feasibility trade-offs. Broadly, novelty here means perceived novelty of a written proposal, not verified/executed discovery — hence the planned end-to-end execution study and the EMNLP-2024 contamination-controlled comparison.
code: https://github.com/NoviScl/AI-Researcher (agent implementation + all human review scores); pre-registration/cached EMNLP ideas at https://osf.io/z6qa4; execution study signup https://tinyurl.com/execution-study
evidence: The paper demonstrates human-perceived novelty (blind expert reviewers rate AI ideas higher on the novelty metric, robustly across three tests and mixed-effects models), which is genuine evidence that outputs are not obviously derivative to experts. BUT the mechanism is pure elicitation of latent LLM knowledge: RAG grounding + massive over-sampling (4000 seed ideas) + reranking, with no learning, environment feedback, or self-evolution. Crucially the paper shows the ceiling of this elicitation: only ~200 unique non-duplicate ideas emerge from 4000, so scaling sampling mostly re-surfaces the same latent ideas rather than producing unbounded novelty. Perceived-novelty is also confounded by subjectivity (inter-reviewer agreement only 56.1%, below NeurIPS'21 66% and ICLR'24 71.9%), and human baseline ideas were self-rated as only ~top-43% of the writers' ideas. No execution or ground-truth-discovery metric is used, so the study cannot distinguish "novel-and-correct invention" from "novel-sounding recombination." Net: strong evidence of perceived novelty, but the underlying process is elicitation, and genuine-discovery value is left unproven.
quotes: we find LLM-generated ideas are judged as more novel (p < 0.05) than human expert ideas while being judged slightly weaker on feasibility.

out of the 4000 generated seed ideas, there are only 200 non-duplicate unique ideas. This sets a bottleneck on our inference-time scaling since increasing the number of generated ideas simply leads to repeating duplicate ideas.

we offer some empirical evidence that LLMs cannot evaluate ideas reliably yet... Even the best LLM evaluator — our own Claude-3.5 pairwise ranker — only achieves an accuracy of 53.3%, lower than our inter-reviewer consistency of 56.1%.
contribution: The first statistically rigorous, large-scale, blind head-to-head comparison of LLM-generated vs. expert-human research ideas, with careful confounder control (matched topics, standardized template, LLM style-normalization). Establishes a human-baseline evaluation protocol and shows AI ideas are judged more novel (p<0.05) across three statistical tests, while diagnosing why the over-generate-and-rank paradigm is limited.
methodology: Recruited 49 expert idea-writers and 79 expert reviewers (104 total, screened for AI-venue publications). Three conditions each with N=49 ideas: (1) Human Ideas, (2) AI Ideas (top-ranked from agent), (3) AI Ideas + Human Rerank. The agent uses Claude-3.5-Sonnet: RAG paper retrieval via Semantic Scholar API (up to 120 papers), generation of 4000 seed ideas per topic, dedup via MiniLM embeddings (cosine>0.8), then a Swiss-tournament pairwise LLM ranker calibrated on 1.2K ICLR-2024 submissions. All ideas normalized to a common style/template; reviewers gave blind 1-10 scores on novelty, excitement, feasibility, effectiveness, overall. Results analyzed with Welch's t-tests (Bonferroni) treating reviews, ideas, and reviewers as datapoints.

title: Nova: An Iterative Planning and Search Approach to Enhance Novelty and Diversity of LLM Generated Ideas
one_line: Nova is an iterative planning-and-search agent that repeatedly plans what external knowledge to retrieve (rather than doing keyword/entity RAG) to generate research ideas that score higher on novelty and diversity than prior idea-generation agents.
key_results: ["3.4x more unique novel ideas vs baseline without the iterative framework (headline)", "At least 2.5x more top-rated ideas than SOTA methods across 170 seed papers (Swiss Tournament)", "Nova produced 619 ideas scored 4 and 2521 ideas scored 5 in the Swiss Tournament, far surpassing baselines", "Over 80% of Nova ideas are unique (Non-Duplicate Percentage), higher than baselines", "Human eval: Nova contributed 37.5% of the top-4 ideas (highest of 4 methods) and only 17.53% of the worst-4 ideas on overall quality; similar pattern for novelty", "Ablation unique-idea counts by step: full Nova grows; no-planning stagnates (step2 42.4 -> step3 44.1); no planning+no retrieval 25.3 -> 30.6 -> 31.35", "Human and automatic rankings agree on method ordering: Nova > AI-Scientist > ResearchAgent > AI-Researcher"]
how_it_relates: Directly relevant as a method whose entire value proposition is squeezing more "novel"/"diverse" ideas out of LLMs via better orchestration and retrieval, not via new learning. The mechanism (plan retrieval -> feed retrieved papers + internal knowledge -> generate -> self-reflect) is a sophisticated pipeline over frozen LLMs. Its own novelty metric is definitionally elicitation-relative: an idea counts as "novel" only if a retriever finds no similar existing paper (embedding cosine < 0.3), i.e. novelty = not-obviously-in-the-retrieved-corpus, which does not distinguish genuine invention from recombination of latent knowledge plus retrieved context. This makes Nova a strong exemplar of the "better exploitation/elicitation" side and a caution about how "novelty" gets operationalized.
datasets: 170 seed papers total: initial corpus 7,805 papers from CVPR 2024, ACL 2024, ICLR 2024; filtered by 'LLM' keywords to ~2,000; cut by citation thresholds (>=20 for ICLR 2024, >=10 for CVPR/ACL 2024) to 153; plus 17 papers from Hugging Face Daily Papers by user rating
Each of the 170 papers used to generate 100 ideas for evaluation (400 ideas per input paper across 4 methods)
Human-eval subset: 20 manually selected papers from ACL/CVPR/ICLR 2024 across NLP, CV, and general LLM fields
arXiv retrieval corpus (AI/NLP/CV categories), time range Jan 1 2022 to Aug 2024, used for novelty/diversity checking
baselines: AI-Researcher (Si et al., 2024)
AI-Scientist (Lu et al., 2024)
ResearchAgent (Baek et al., 2024)
Ablations: Nova without planning, and Nova without both planning and retrieval (K=5 papers retrieved for controlled comparison)
metrics: Quality: Swiss System Tournament score with Claude-3.5-Sonnet zero-shot pairwise ranker, 5 rounds per idea (score 0-5)

Novelty (automatic): LLM judges whether an idea is novel by checking top-10 most relevant retrieved papers; considered novel if no retrieved paper is 'similar', where similarity = all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embedding cosine > 0.3 (following Baek et al. 2024 / Si et al. 2024)
Diversity: proportion of unique (non-duplicate) ideas, duplicate threshold cosine 0.8 (Non-Duplicate Percentage)
Human evaluation: 10 PhD/professor experts rank best-4/worst-4 among 20 ideas per paper on Novelty, Feasibility, Effectiveness, Overall; tracked which methods produce top-20% of ideas
limitations: ["No continuous improvement beyond 3 iterations (novelty/diversity gains plateau after 3 rounds)", "Planning uses only LLMs' internal knowledge with no reward function, which may limit planning effectiveness (future work: add reward function)", "Novelty/diversity are automated proxy metrics (retrieval + embedding thresholds), not validated as genuine scientific breakthrough; ideas are proposals only and never implemented or empirically tested", "Risk of idea homogenization / narrower perspectives from wide LLM use, acknowledged in ethics section", "Evaluation confounds: LLM-based ranker (Claude-3.5-Sonnet) evaluates ideas; human panel is small (10 experts, best/worst-4 ranking rather than absolute scoring)"]
code: none found
evidence: The paper never demonstrates creation of genuinely new science; it demonstrates that a planning/search wrapper over frozen LLMs produces more items that pass an automated novelty check and win pairwise-quality tournaments. Gains come entirely from orchestration: seed diversification (10 discovery templates, trend tracking), LLM-planned retrieval of external papers, iterative 3x expansion, self-reflection, and k-means deduplication. The ablation shows that without external retrieval/planning, unique-idea counts stagnate (25.3->30.6->31.35), i.e. improvements are driven by injecting retrieved context and by the search wrapper, not by new capability. "Novelty" is measured as absence of a similar paper among the top-10 retrieved (embedding threshold 0.3) and diversity as proportion of non-duplicate ideas (threshold 0.8) — both are proxies for "not redundant with retrieved corpus," not for genuine conceptual breakthrough. Evaluation is LLM/human preference ranking of idea proposals; no idea is implemented or empirically validated. The paper itself frames the method as leveraging the LLM's internal knowledge to plan retrieval, i.e. better exploitation of existing knowledge (both internal and external), which is elicitation/prompt-optimization in spirit rather than novel-concept generation.
quotes: Nova leverages the internal knowledge of LLMs to generate search plans for external knowledge retrieval, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of the retrieval process.

we use LLMs to judge whether a generated idea is novel by checking the top 10 most relevant papers ... and if no paper is identified as containing a similar idea, it is considered novel ... if the cosine similarity threshold is larger than 0.3, we say that they are similar.
To mimic human intuition, we tap into the LLMs' internal knowledge to craft initial seed ideas.
contribution: Introduces an iterative planning framework for LLM research-idea generation in which the LLM plans which knowledge to retrieve in each round, progressively broadening/deepening search; reports 3.4x more unique novel ideas vs no-framework baseline and at least 2.5x more top-rated ideas than SOTA agents across 170 seed papers.
