methodology: Three-stage pipeline: (1) multi-source seed idea generation from an input paper using references, a "knowledge tracking" module of trending recent papers (via social/GitHub engagement), 10 fundamental scientific-discovery methods (drawn from Kuhn's paradigm), LLM internal knowledge, plus self-check/self-critique/reflection, yielding 15 seed ideas; (2) T=3 iterations of planning-and-search where the LLM devises a search plan to retrieve papers that enhance novelty/diversity, generating 10 new ideas per idea then self-reflecting down to 3 (3x growth per iteration), replacing old seeds; (3) cluster 405 ideas via k-means into 100 clusters and expand cluster centers into detailed initial/final proposals (templates following Si et al. 2024).

title: HINDSIGHT: Evaluating LLM-Generated Research Ideas via Future Impact
one_line: A time-split evaluation framework that scores LLM-generated research ideas by how well they match real, high-impact papers published after the generator's knowledge cutoff, revealing that LLM judges reward "novel-sounding" ideas that rarely anticipate actual research.
key_results: HINDSIGHT: RA vs baseline mean 0.297 vs 0.119 (+0.178, p<0.001), a 2.5x gap; match rate 81% vs 42%; avg matches 9.0 vs 3.3. LLM-as-Judge Overall: 7.44 vs 7.40 (+0.03, p=0.584, n.s.); baseline rated HIGHER on Novelty (7.11 vs 6.68, p<0.001) and Impact (8.17 vs 7.87, p<0.001); RA higher only on Feasibility (7.68 vs 7.09). Correlation (RA): HINDSIGHT vs Novelty rho=-0.291 (p=0.003), vs Impact rho=-0.225 (p=0.025), vs Feasibility rho=+0.252 (p=0.012); baseline correlations all non-significant. Threshold sensitivity: RA/BL mean-score ratio grows monotonically from 1.1x (theta=0.93) to 3.8x (theta=0.965). Example match: an RA idea matched IP-Adapter (1,397 citations) at similarity 0.977.
how_it_relates: This paper is squarely a measurement/evaluation contribution for the core question. It does not build a self-evolving agent; instead it offers an objective yardstick for whether generated ideas are "genuinely anticipatory" (aligned with real future research) versus merely "novel-sounding." Its central empirical finding is a warning directly relevant to the novelty-vs-elicitation debate: the ideas an LLM judge rates as most novel are the LEAST likely to correspond to real future work (rho=-0.29), implying that subjective novelty signals reward speculative framing rather than genuine discovery. It also cautions that optimizing idea generators against LLM-judge scores may push toward "impressive-sounding but ultimately vacuous ideas" — i.e., a form of glorified prompt/score optimization. Importantly, its own "conformity bias" limitation flags that matching published work cannot by construction credit truly revolutionary ideas (e.g., inventing a wholly new paradigm), so it measures anticipation of real trends, not radical invention.
datasets: Post-T ground truth pool: 27,589 unique AI/ML papers from Semantic Scholar API, published June 2023 - December 2025, across 10 research topics (with titles, abstracts, citation counts, venue info)
200 generated ideas: 100 from ResearchAgent (retrieval-augmented), 100 from vanilla baseline; 10 topics x 10 seeds/ideas each
Pre-T literature retrieved via Semantic Scholar API restricted to before June 2023
baselines: Vanilla baseline idea generator: same LLM (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) prompted with only topic name + generic instruction, no literature retrieval
ResearchAgent (retrieval-augmented, simplified 2-stage reimplementation) as the contrasting system
LLM-as-Judge (Qwen3-32B) as the competing/incumbent evaluation method being critiqued
metrics: HINDSIGHT score (max impact over matched future papers; impact = 0.6*normalized citations + 0.4*top-venue indicator)
Match rate (% of ideas matching >=1 ground truth paper) and average number of matches
LLM-as-Judge dimensions (1-10): Novelty, Feasibility, Expected Impact, Overall
Spearman correlation (rho) between HINDSIGHT and each LLM-Judge dimension
Mann-Whitney U test p-values; quadrant classification (True Positive, Hidden Gem, Overhyped, True Negative)
limitations: Authors list: (1) Scale — only 200 ideas across 10 topics, two systems. (2) Single embedding model — SPECTER2 base without adapter heads may miss structural/methodological novelty expressed in different terminology. (3) Ground truth incompleteness — 27,589-paper pool does not cover all AI/ML; unmatched ideas get false-zero scores. (4) Threshold sensitivity — absolute scores depend on theta=0.96. (5) Conformity bias — rewards proximity to published work, potentially undervaluing genuinely novel/revolutionary ideas (the key caveat for the "genuine novelty" question). (6) Single judge model (Qwen3-32B). Additional: 30-month window may miss slower-to-materialize ideas; topic-level semantic matching only.
code: none found
evidence: The paper demonstrates a validated proxy metric for "idea anticipates real future research" but explicitly does NOT measure genuine paradigm-shifting novelty. Evidence for its stance: (1) HINDSIGHT distinguishes retrieval-augmented from vanilla generation (0.297 vs 0.119 mean, 81% vs 42% match rate, p<0.001) whereas LLM-as-Judge overall does not (7.44 vs 7.40, p=0.584), showing subjective novelty scores fail to track real impact. (2) Negative correlation between HINDSIGHT and LLM-judged novelty (rho=-0.29, p=0.003) and impact (rho=-0.22), with positive correlation only for feasibility (rho=+0.25) — i.e., grounded/incremental-looking ideas match real work while grand/vague ones ("holistic frameworks", "unified approaches") match nothing. This is evidence that LLM "novelty" is largely surface framing, not discovery. However, the framework's ground-truth-matching design inherently rewards conformity to what WAS published, so by the authors' own admission it "potentially undervalu[es] truly revolutionary ideas that open entirely new directions" — it cannot certify genuine novelty, only anticipation of realized trends.
quotes: The negative correlation between HINDSIGHT and LLM-judged novelty suggests that language models reward 'novel-sounding' framing over genuinely anticipatory thinking.

HINDSIGHT rewards matching published work, potentially undervaluing truly revolutionary ideas that open entirely new directions. We view this as an acceptable trade-off: the framework measures anticipation of real research trends, which is meaningful even if incomplete.

If LLM judges consistently fail to distinguish retrieval-augmented from vanilla generation, then optimizing idea generation systems against LLM judge scores may be misguided—it could push systems toward producing impressive-sounding but ultimately vacuous ideas.
contribution: Introduces HINDSIGHT, described as the first time-split, impact-grounded evaluation framework for research idea generation. It provides empirical evidence of a systematic disconnect between LLM-as-Judge scores and objective future-impact evaluation, and shows that LLM judges overvalue novel-sounding ideas while undervaluing ideas that anticipate real research trends (negative correlation between HINDSIGHT and LLM-judged novelty).
methodology: Fix a temporal cutoff T (June 2023) below the generator LLM's knowledge cutoff (Llama-3.3-70B, Dec 2023 cutoff, 6-month margin). Restrict the idea-generation system to pre-T literature, then treat papers published in the following 30 months (post-T pool of 27,589 Semantic Scholar AI/ML papers) as ground truth. Each generated idea (problem + method) and each paper (title + abstract) is encoded with SPECTER2 (768-dim), matched via a FAISS cosine-similarity index (top-K=20, threshold theta=0.96). Matched papers are impact-scored as h(p)=0.6*normalized_citations + 0.4*top_venue_indicator; the idea's HINDSIGHT score is the max impact over its matches (0 if no match). Compared against LLM-as-Judge (Qwen3-32B, 4 dimensions, 3 runs averaged).

title: Library Drift: Diagnosing and Fixing a Silent Failure Mode in Self-Evolving LLM Skill Libraries
one_line: A skeptical diagnosis showing that self-evolving LLM skill libraries silently degrade ("library drift") without outcome-driven lifecycle governance, and that even with a working fix the "skills" are just curated natural-language prompt snippets that recover latent model capability rather than create new knowledge.
key_results: Default (full governance) lifts held-out pass@1 from 0.258 baseline to late-window mean 0.584 (peak 0.658), rolling gain +0.328 +/-0.018 over 100 rounds. A1 no-injection floor: +0.002 +/-0.005 (bank still has 42 active skills, 0 injected). A4 harsh retirement: -0.019 +/-0.010 (below baseline; bank collapses to 2 active skills; router engagement drops to 19%). Healthy conditions maintain 70-80% router engagement. Meta-skill removal (A3) costs 43-57% of gain (single most valuable component). Explicit dedup (A5 +0.374, A6 +0.363) slightly exceeds Default, i.e. unnecessary given meta-skill. Cost: ~14.5k LLM calls / 6.5h per 100 rounds vs 10k / 2.3h for no-skill baseline. Motivating external number: SkillsBench LLM-authored skills +0.0pp vs human-curated +16.2pp.
how_it_relates: Strongly supports the "elicitation, not novelty" side. The entire gain comes from routing/injecting natural-language guidance into a frozen model's prompt: skills are never validated as new capabilities, and A1 shows that skill *creation* alone (synthesis running but no injection) yields +0.002 = zero gain, so all value is in the prompt-injection/routing act, i.e. the loop is a form of curated prompt optimization. Tasks are pre-filtered to ones "where a skill library can plausibly help" (Opus fails some of 5 seeds), and the recipe roughly doubles pass@1 on those already-solvable-by-some-seeds tasks, consistent with re-eliciting latent competence rather than inventing anything. The paper's own framing ("the bottleneck is not the author; it is the librarian") reframes self-evolution as a memory-management problem, not a discovery problem. It also documents that naive self-evolution actively harms (drift), a key cautionary data point for the review.
datasets: MBPP+ hard-100 (100 tasks sampled from the 378-task MBPP+ test split after discarding ~273 tasks Claude Opus 4.7 solves on all 5 baseline seeds; 60 train / 40 eval)
baselines: No-skill baseline (round-0 pass@1, ~0.258)
A1 no skill injection (no-skill floor, +0.002)
A2 retrieval-only routing / no LLM gate (+0.077)
A3 no meta-skill (+0.187)
A4 harsh retirement (-0.019)
A5 no canonicalisation (+0.374)
A6 no cover-guard (+0.363)
A8 meta-synth refresh (+0.372)
Prior systems named but not re-run: Voyager, ExpeL, AutoManual
metrics: Held-out pass@1 (primary end-task)
Gain = mean(last 10 rounds) - mean(first 10 rounds) of held-out pass@1
Per-skill contribution score c_hat(s) = (successes-failures)/trials (drift diagnostic)
Attribution verdicts: HELPED/HURT/NEUTRAL/INAPPLICABLE proportions (early-warning drift signal)
Router engagement (% eval tasks assigned a skill vs NONE)
Active/Retired bank size, Critic LLM verdict-call count
Operational cost: LLM calls, wall time
limitations: Authors state: (i) single benchmark (MBPP+ hard-100); generalization to broader/multi-step domains (SWE-Bench) left to future work; (ii) single model (Claude Opus 4.7), no cross-model stability; (iii) diagnostic intervention thresholds are empirically chosen, not principled; (iv) claim that drift signals sharpen in multi-step agents is only hypothesized. Additional (reviewer) limitations for the core question: workshop paper (ICML 2026 FAGEN workshop); tasks are pre-filtered to be skill-amenable, inflating apparent gains; no novelty/discovery metric at all; "skills" are natural-language prompt snippets so results speak to prompt curation, not knowledge creation; small n (3 seeds, 40 eval tasks) with wide variance; the non-divergence bound is admittedly loose.
code: none found
evidence: The paper never claims or measures creation of new knowledge; it measures pass@1 recovery on a curated hard subset. A1 (full synthesis pipeline runs but Router forced to NONE so no skill is injected) gives +0.002, proving skill *authoring* contributes nothing without prompt injection - the gain is entirely an injection/routing effect. A2 (retrieval-only, no LLM gate) gets +0.077 vs Default +0.328, showing the LLM gate (deciding when to inject) is the main lever. The "meta-skill authoring prior" (a stylistic prompt) is the single most valuable component (removing it costs 43-57% of gain), underscoring that stylistic prompt shaping, not discovered content, drives results. The cited SkillsBench headline (LLM-authored skills +0.0pp vs human-curated +16.2pp) is the motivating evidence that LLM self-authored skills add nothing absent human curation. There is no novelty metric, no held-out-domain transfer, no measure that skills encode information the base model lacked.
quotes: Self-evolving skill libraries face a silent failure mode we term library drift: unbounded skill accumulation without outcome-driven lifecycle management causes retrieval degradation, false-positive injections, and performance stagnation.
LLM-authored skills deliver +0.0pp over no-skill baselines while human-curated ones deliver +16.2pp... The libraries grow, but the agents do not improve.
A1 (no skill injection)... Result: +0.002 ... This isolates the routing effect: skill creation alone, without injection, produces no gain.
The bottleneck in self-evolving skill libraries is not the author; it is the librarian.
contribution: Defines "library drift" as a first-class failure mode of self-evolving skill libraries (accumulated artifacts push performance below the no-skill baseline), provides two reproducible ablation triggers that bracket it (A1 no-injection floor +0.002; A4 harsh-retirement active harm -0.019), introduces trace-level diagnostics (per-skill contribution scores, HELPED/HURT attribution verdicts, router engagement) that detect drift before end-task scores fall, and delivers a minimal governance recipe ("Ratchet") plus 8 ablations identifying which mechanisms are load-bearing.
methodology: Ratchet is a single frozen-LLM loop (Router->Solver->Grader->Capsule; then Critic->Synthesizer->Curator) that writes, retrieves, curates, and retires its own natural-language skills across 100 rounds. Three governance mechanisms prevent drift: outcome-driven retirement (retire skill when n>=Nmin=100 trials and empirical contribution c<=-tau=0.10), a bounded active-cap (C=50, evict lowest-contribution skill), and a meta-skill authoring prior constraining the Synthesizer. New skills are born only from clusters of >=3 failures sharing a canonical pattern. A non-divergence proposition bounds expected pass@1 above no-skill floor minus (tau+eps+C*delta). Evaluated on MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7, Cohere embed-v4, 3 seeds.

title: SkillsBench: Benchmarking How Well Agent Skills Work Across Diverse Tasks
one_line: A paired (with-Skills vs. no-Skills) benchmark of 87 expertise-heavy containerized tasks showing that human-curated procedural Skills raise agent pass rates by +16.6 pp on average, while agent-self-generated Skills actually hurt performance, falling below the no-Skills baseline.
key_results: Curated Skills raise average pass rate from 33.9% to 50.5% (+16.6 pp; 25.5% normalized gain); all 18 configurations improved, range +4.1 to +25.7 pp. Best with-Skills systems: OpenHands+GPT-5.5 67.3%, Codex+GPT-5.5 66.5%, Claude Code+Opus 4.7 61.2%. Largest lifts: OpenHands+GLM 5.1 +25.7 pp, Gemini CLI+Gemini 3.1 Pro +24.8 pp. Self-generated Skills FELL BELOW no-Skills baseline: -8.1 pp (Claude Code+Opus 4.7), -11.3 pp (Codex+GPT-5.5), -11.5 pp (Gemini CLI+Gemini 3.1 Pro). Domain gains: Natural Science +28.8, Media +24.1, Cybersecurity +18.9, ... Software Eng +11.6, Math/OR +9.7. Skill design: 1 skill +18.0 pp, 2-3 skills +19.0 pp, >=4 skills only +10.1 pp; compact/standard skills +19.0/+21.5 pp vs comprehensive prose +0.7 pp. Top task: llm-prefix-cache-replay 1.9% -> 94.4%; dapt-intrusion-detection 0% -> 81.5%. 13/87 tasks show negative deltas. Small model with skills (MiniMax M2.7 34.9%) beats larger models without (GLM 5.1 32.7%, MiniMax M3 29.7%).
how_it_relates: Directly central to the elicitation-vs-novelty question through its Condition C (self-generated Skills) result. When agents author their OWN procedural knowledge and then use it, they perform WORSE than with no skills at all (-8.1 to -11.5 pp), whereas independently human-curated Skills add +18.2 to +24.8 pp on the same configurations. This is strong evidence that, in current systems, the value of Skills comes from externally injected human expertise the model did not already possess and could not regenerate for itself — not from an agent bootstrapping genuinely new procedural knowledge. The domain-level finding reinforces this: gains are largest exactly where procedural knowledge is underrepresented in pretraining (Natural Science +28.8, Cybersecurity +18.9) and smallest where models already have strong priors (Software Engineering +11.6, Math/OR +9.7), suggesting Skills fill knowledge gaps rather than create new capability the model could elicit on its own. However, the paper measures task success, not idea novelty, and its own limitations section admits gains could partly be a "more context" effect rather than genuinely new procedural structure.
datasets: SkillsBench: 87 tasks across 8 domains (Software Engineering 16, Industrial & Physical Systems 14, Natural Science 14, Office & White Collar 14, Finance & Economics 9, Mathematics & OR 8, Cybersecurity 7, Media & Content Production 5)
Difficulty strata: 6 Core (<60 min), 53 Extended (1-4 h), 28 Extreme (>4 h)
Drawn from 400 candidate submissions by 142 contributors (grown from a 1,400-member community, 200+ onboarded authors)
Skill ecosystem construction snapshot: 2,014,000 deduplicated source-partitioned Skills (234,896 GitHub/source + 1,773,213 marketplace + 5,891 partner); 767,430 bundles cloned for structural stats
baselines: No-Skills condition (task instruction only) — the primary matched baseline (rA)
Curated-Skills condition (expert bundle mounted, must be discovered) (rB)
Self-generated Skills condition (agent authors packs via Anthropic skill-creator, then solves) (rC)

18 model-harness configurations spanning OpenHands, Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI and models incl. GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7/4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GLM 5.1, DeepSeek V4, Kimi K2.6, MiniMax M2.7/M3, Grok 4.3
metrics: Task-macro pass rate (per-task pass/fail averaged over 3 trials, then macro-averaged over 87 tasks; Terminal-Bench scoring)
Absolute gain Delta (pp)
Normalized gain g = (pass_skill - pass_vanilla)/(1 - pass_vanilla) [Hake 1998] = fraction of remaining headroom closed
Skill Invocation Rate (fraction of curated trials whose trajectory reads/invokes a task-specific Skill)
No explicit novelty or discovery metric — measures task resolution only
limitations: Coverage limited to terminal-based containerized tasks (may not transfer to GUI/multi-agent/very-long-horizon). Causal attribution weak: Skills add context length, so gains "could partly reflect 'more context' rather than procedural structure" — no length-matched random/irrelevant-text baseline was run (flagged as future work). Self-generated deficit confounds content quality with skill-discovery and creator/solver interference. Containerization does not guarantee determinism or immunity to training-set leakage/memorization. Limited/evolving set of models and harnesses. Uses curated (high-quality) Skills, not ecosystem-representative or automatically selected lower-quality Skills. Skill composition effects not studied.
code: Open-sourced benchmark, BenchFlow harness, and public trajectories/results (BenchFlow: https://github.com/benchflow-ai/benchflow; correspondence xiangyi@benchflow.ai); arXiv:2602.12670v4
evidence: The paper demonstrates that curated Skills close a fraction of remaining headroom (25.5% normalized gain) on expertise-heavy tasks, but the load-bearing evidence for the core question is the self-generated arm: agents authoring their own skills land BELOW the no-Skills baseline on all three tested configurations, while human-curated skills add ~+18-25 pp. This shows the benefit is not something the model can produce for itself — it depends on injecting pre-existing human expertise. The trajectory audit attributes self-generated failure to "creator-side authoring that displaces solver work" and "confidently wrong pack content." The paper frames Skills as filling procedural gaps ("Skills close procedural gaps... most helpful when success depends on concrete procedures and verifier-facing details... smaller or negative when models already have strong priors"), which is fundamentally an elicitation/knowledge-injection story, not novel-discovery. Crucially, the paper does NOT claim or measure creation of new ideas; it measures task resolution rate. It explicitly flags a confound: "Skills injection increases context length, so observed gains could partly reflect 'more context' rather than procedural structure," and calls for length-matched random/irrelevant-text baselines it did not run. Net: the paper is evidence that Skills = better exploitation of injected (human) knowledge, and that current agents cannot self-generate that value.
quotes: Self-generated Skills land below the no-Skills baseline on all three configurations (-8.1 pp on Claude Code + Opus 4.7, -11.3 pp on Codex + GPT-5.5, -11.5 pp on Gemini CLI + Gemini 3.1 Pro), while curated Skills add +18.2 to +24.8 pp on the same configurations.

Skills are most helpful when success depends on concrete procedures and verifier-facing details (steps, constraints, sanity checks), rather than broad conceptual knowledge... smaller or negative when models already have strong priors.

Skills injection increases context length, so observed gains could partly reflect 'more context' rather than procedural structure... Future work requires stronger length-matched baselines, such as random or irrelevant text and retrieval-only documentation.
contribution: Introduces SkillsBench, the first benchmark treating Agent Skills as a first-class evaluation artifact. Two-tier contribution: (1) a quantitative measurement of "how much do Skills help?" via matched paired conditions, and (2) a general paired-evaluation framework (with vs. without augmentation on the identical task/container) with contributor-driven sourcing and leakage-controlled task-to-Skill decoupling, portable to other augmentations (RAG, memory, scaffolding).
methodology: Each task is a containerized unit with fixed data, an oracle solution, and a deterministic pytest verifier. Every task is run under matched conditions differing only in Skill access: Condition A (instruction only), Condition B (curated expert-authored Skills bundle mounted, agent must discover them via progressive disclosure — Skills are never named in the instruction), and Condition C (self-generated: the agent first authors its own skill packs with Anthropic's skill-creator, then solves using only those). Efficacy is a paired delta at the (config, task) level: Delta_curated = rB - rA and Delta_self = rC - rA. Skills are contributor-authored independently of the benchmark and pass four automated leakage gates (rejecting task-specific filenames, magic numbers, verbatim oracle commands, expected outputs) so a Skill provides class-level expertise, never an instance answer key. Evaluated on the BenchFlow harness across 18 model-harness configurations (OpenHands, Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI), 3 trials per cell.

title: Do LLM-Generated Skills Make Better AI Data Scientists? A Component Ablation Across Data-Science Workflows
one_line: A large ablation study finding that low-curation, LLM-generated "skill" files provide no reliable improvement over task-only prompting for data-science agents, and perform no better than a token-matched irrelevant-content control.
key_results: Aggregate spread across all 5 conditions only 1.2 pp (67.3%-68.5%); No-Skill=68.3%, Full=67.5%. No condition significant (all p>=0.396; mixed-model betas: Full -0.008 p=0.497, Core+Refs -0.010 p=0.396). Full vs token-matched Length-Control: +0.6 pp, p=0.775 (indistinguishable from irrelevant content). Full+Priority=68.7% (vs Full +1.2 pp p=0.362; vs No-Skill +0.4 pp p=0.875). By stage: Data Extraction 88.9->91.3 (ceiling), Reporting 97.6->99.2 (ceiling), Data Preparation 57.1->50.8 (skill HARMS), Stat. Analysis 29.4->28.6 (floor). On the 17 informative (30-80% baseline) tasks all skill conditions trend negative (Full -2.6 pp, Core-Only -5.2 pp). Full skills cost ~4.5x input tokens (mean 1,293 vs 287) with no accuracy gain. McNemar Core+Examples: 20 helped vs 19 hurt, p=1.000.
how_it_relates: This is a strong negative/critical data point for the elicitation side of the core question, specifically for the simplest form of "self-authored" skills. When an LLM generates its own reusable guidance and injects it, it adds nothing beyond the task prompt: Full skills match a token-matched IRRELEVANT control (+0.6 pp, p=0.775), meaning any small effect is from prompt length/format, not from useful new knowledge. The paper's "Redundancy at ceiling" pattern directly supports elicitation: on SQL/JSON tasks the model already has the capability from pretraining, so the skill is redundant. Crucially, the paper shows LLM-generated skills fail to create genuinely useful new procedural knowledge, and can even HARM (conflicting generic heuristics override task-specific instructions). It contrasts with SkillsBench's finding that HUMAN-curated skills add +16.2 pp, implying the value comes from human novelty/curation, not from the LLM's self-generated content, i.e., LLMs cannot bootstrap novel useful skills for themselves here.
datasets: 56 custom tasks, 14 per stage: Data Preparation (CSV cleaning: null imputation, dedup, type coercion, date standardization) on Melbourne Housing, Titanic, UCI Wine Quality, Auto MPG, student performance
Data Extraction (text-to-SQL) using Spider schemas and Chinook/Northwind-style databases
Statistical Analysis (hypothesis testing, effect sizes) on Fisher Iris, mtcars, PlantGrowth, ToothGrowth, sleep
Reporting (structured JSON from APIs) using GitHub Events, OpenWeatherMap, REST Countries
Task split: 28 self-authored (Gemini-drafted) + 28 externally grounded
baselines: No-Skill (task prompt only) - primary baseline
Core-Only (Routing + Core Procedure)
Core+Examples
Core+Refs
Full skill (all 4 sections)
Length-Control (token-matched task-irrelevant office-supply skill)
Full+Priority (Full skill + task-over-skill directive)
metrics: Binary pass/fail from deterministic verifiers (aggregated by 2-of-3 majority vote per cell)
Pass rate (%) per model/condition/stage
Percentage-point (pp) difference vs No-Skill with bootstrap 95% CIs
Mixed-effects model coefficients (beta) and p-values
McNemar help/hurt flip counts
Input/output token footprint (cost)
NOTE: no explicit novelty or discovery metric is used; the paper measures task correctness, not idea novelty
limitations: Narrow scope: 56 single-turn tasks in one domain, only 17 in the informative 30-80% band; CIs span +/-4-6 pp so can rule out moderate but not small (1-3 pp) effects. Tests only single-shot flat-file injection, not multi-turn planning, tool use, code execution, memory, iterative revision, selective loading, system-level priority, or schema/tool constraints. No expert-written/task-specific positive-control skill, so benchmark sensitivity to genuinely good skills is unmeasured. One generated skill per stage (4 instances), so section effects are tied to few skill samples with no inter-generation variance estimate. Authorship overlap: Gemini 2.5 Pro helped author skills and self-authored tasks/gold outputs and is also an evaluated model. Condition-flip mechanism analysis is diagnostic only.
code: none found (evaluation harness referenced but no public code/data URL given)
evidence: The token-matched control is the decisive evidence: Full generated data-science skills perform statistically indistinguishably from task-IRRELEVANT office-supply content of equal length (Full vs Length-Control = +0.6 pp, 95% CI [-2.2,+3.4], p=0.775). This means the generated skill content carries no measurable useful information beyond adding formatted text, i.e., no new capability is being created. The "Redundancy at ceiling" pattern (SQL 88.9%, reporting 97.6% baselines) shows the model already possesses these capabilities from pretraining, leaving no room for a skill to add anything, consistent with pure elicitation of latent knowledge. Where baselines are moderate (data prep 57.1%), skills mostly HURT (-2.3 to -6.3 pp) because generic self-generated heuristics conflict with the actual task, showing the LLM-generated content is not creating correct new procedure. The contrast with human-curated skills (+16.2 pp in cited SkillsBench) implies whatever value exists comes from human input, not LLM self-generation. Caveat: no expert/positive-control skill was included, so the study cannot show the benchmark WOULD detect a genuinely good skill; and it tests only single-shot flat injection, not iterative/feedback-based skill learning.
quotes: Compared with prompting using the task alone, neither the full generated skill nor any ablated skill variant significantly improves performance; all p-values are at least 0.396, and the total spread across variants is only 1.2 pp.
SkillsBench (86 tasks, 7,308 trajectories) found that human-curated skills improve performance substantially (+16.2 pp), while LLM-generated skills provide no aggregate benefit.
In these stages, skill content appears mostly redundant with behavior the model already has in this benchmark.
contribution: Tests whether one LLM-generated SKILL.md per data-science lifecycle stage helps agents; ablates the skill into components (Routing, Core Procedure, Worked Examples, Reference Notes); and adds a token-matched irrelevant-content control plus a task-over-skill priority directive. Finds no reliable benefit from any full or ablated skill variant, and characterizes three failure regimes.
methodology: One skill per lifecycle stage (data preparation, data extraction/SQL, statistical analysis, reporting) was generated autonomously by a Gemini 2.5 Pro coding agent with no human editing (the "low-curation" workflow), each with four sections. Skills are injected as a flat user-message prefix; ablation deletes sections mechanically to make 5 conditions (No-Skill, Core-Only, Core+Examples, Core+Refs, Full). Evaluated over 56 tasks x 9 model configs (3 providers) x 3 reps = 7,560 main runs, plus 1,512 runs each for a token-matched Length-Control (irrelevant "office-supply" skill within 1% token length) and a Full+Priority directive. Outcomes are binary pass/fail from deterministic verifiers; analyzed with mixed-effects models, bootstrap CIs, and McNemar tests.

title: Reasoning or Reciting? Exploring the Capabilities and Limitations of Language Models Through Counterfactual Tasks
one_line: By evaluating LLMs on "counterfactual" variants of familiar tasks (same reasoning procedure, altered input-output mapping, e.g. base-9 arithmetic), the paper shows models degrade consistently and substantially relative to default conditions, evidencing reliance on memorized, default-condition-specific behavior rather than abstract, transferable reasoning.
key_results: Counterfactual performance is consistently and substantially lower than default across all 11 tasks and 4 models, both with and without 0-CoT, while CCC accuracy is usually high (esp. for GPT-4). Arithmetic can drop from near-perfect to near-zero moving off base-10. "Commonness" matters: bases 8/16 beat 9/11; drop-D tuning gives GPT-4 its best counterfactual chord score; north-south axis swap degrades least (matplotlib/ggplot/D3 invert y-axis), sometimes even exceeding default for PaLM-2. Digit-scaling: base-10 addition accuracy decays far slower than other bases as operand length grows (memorization signature). Few-shot: up to 16 demonstrations reduce but do not eliminate the base-9/11/16 gap, and gains plateau by 16-shot. Logic: LLMs score higher with more true premises and when conclusion truthfulness matches the label (statistically significant), indicating reliance on real-world priors over symbolic deduction.
how_it_relates: This is a foundational diagnostic for the core question. Self-evolving/Skills agents built on LLMs risk producing outputs that merely re-instantiate patterns memorized from pretraining (the "default" world). The counterfactual methodology operationalizes a test: a system possessing genuine, generalizable abstractions (the paper's explicit analogy is "physicists using mathematical abstractions to make predictions about universes substantially different from our own") should transfer to novel conditions with little degradation, whereas a system that has only found a better way to elicit memorized default-condition behavior will collapse when the world is shifted off the default. It also directly addresses the prompt-optimization worry: they argue CoT and few-shot demonstrations reduce but do NOT eliminate the gap, suggesting the deficit is not merely a prompting artifact. Thus it supplies a measurement lever for distinguishing "new" from "elicited," while cautioning that reasoning and reciting are a continuum, not a dichotomy.
datasets: 11 counterfactual task suite (arithmetic in bases 8/9/11/16; spatial reasoning with swapped/rotated/permuted axes; drawing rotated/flipped objects in Processing; music: chord fingering with scordatura tunings and note retrieval under transposition; chess opening legality under Chess960; SET game with inverted number rule; first-order logic; basic syntactic reasoning; code execution; code generation)
HumanEval (code execution/generation, evaluated under fictional 1-based-indexing 'ThonPy')
FOLIO (first-order logic; premises manually rewritten to violate commonsense)
synthetic word-order-permuted English (subject-verb-object vs permuted orders, from dependency-tree manipulation) for syntactic reasoning
an existing guitar/ukulele chord database for chord fingering
all data synthetically generated and released
baselines: GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-0301)
Claude (claude-v1.3)
PaLM-2 (text-bison-001, not the largest version)
each tested with and without zero-shot chain-of-thought ('Let's think step by step')
few-shot in-context demonstrations (up to 16-shot) as an additional condition for arithmetic
random-chance baseline reported per task
metrics: default-task accuracy vs counterfactual-task accuracy (and the gap between them as the key diagnostic of overfitting/reciting)
Counterfactual Comprehension Check (CCC) accuracy — controls for surface understanding of the altered rules
above-random-vs-random performance
correlation between default and counterfactual performance (evidence of a reasoning-reciting continuum)
logistic-regression coefficients predicting per-instance correctness from premise truthfulness features (logic probe)
instance-level all-objects-correct accuracy (spatial, ~16.7% random)
limitations: Counterfactual worlds are only "less common," not truly alien, so counterfactual-ness is relative. Non-perfect CCC accuracy means default-vs-counterfactual gaps cannot perfectly disentangle reasoning failure from failure to comprehend the new rules (though gaps are often so large they remain strongly suggestive). Some tasks admit a "revert-to-default" shortcut — e.g. the model could map counterfactual tokens back to the default (headphones→dogs in logic) or invert a drawing transform — which they could disallow for drawing but not for logic, inflating counterfactual scores there. Only closed models with unknown size/data were tested; the largest PaLM-2 was inaccessible. They cannot tractably rule out that some cleverer prompt could close the gap, and left more advanced prompting (self-consistency, ToT, etc.) to future work. Reasoning vs reciting is a continuum, so results bound but do not zero out genuine reasoning.
code: https://github.com/ZhaofengWu/counterfactual-evaluation (code, all synthetic data, and LM prompts/responses released)
evidence: The paper does not itself demonstrate LLMs creating anything genuinely new; rather it demonstrates the opposite tendency and gives a method to detect it. Evidence for elicitation/memorization over novel reasoning: (1) consistent, substantial counterfactual degradation across 11 tasks and 4 models even when CCC accuracy is high (arithmetic drops from near-perfect to near-zero in non-base-10); (2) a "commonness" effect where more frequent counterfactual conditions (bases 8/16 vs 9/11; drop-D guitar tuning; matplotlib-style inverted y-axis) yield smaller gaps, a memorization-like signature; (3) in the digit-scaling analysis, base-10 accuracy decays much more slowly than other bases with more digits, consistent with memorized input-output mappings rather than an executed algorithm; (4) in logic, a logistic-regression probe shows LLM correctness depends on premise truthfulness / conclusion-truth-matching-the-label, i.e. reasoning is contaminated by real-world priors instead of pure symbolic deduction; (5) 0-shot CoT and up to 16-shot demonstrations reduce but do not close the gap. Counter-evidence (mixed): above-random counterfactual performance on most tasks and a default-counterfactual correlation indicate SOME transferable reasoning co-exists — the authors explicitly frame reasoning vs reciting as a continuum, not a dichotomy.
quotes: This suggests that these models' ability on these tasks is supported at least in part by non-transferable, default-condition-specific behaviors rather than abstract, generalizable reasoning skills.

an ideal learner with the right inductive biases should be able to structure their internal parameters and representations to implement general-purpose abstractions (e.g., the concept of addition), and use these abstractions to generalize to counterfactual conditions, analogous to physicists using mathematical abstractions to make predictions about universes that are substantially different from our own... LMs trained on large text corpora, remarkable as they may be, are still quite susceptible to overfitting with frequency effects.

given the consistency of the gap across our tasks (which use different prompts) and the 0-shot CoT setting, we believe that a prompt that completely bridges the default-counterfactual gap is unlikely.
contribution: Introduces the counterfactual-task evaluation paradigm as a way to separate genuine, transferable reasoning ("reasoning") from recall/exploitation of frequently-seen default task instances ("reciting"). Provides a suite of 11 counterfactual tasks plus per-task Counterfactual Comprehension Checks (CCCs) that control for the confound of the model simply not understanding the altered rules.
methodology: Take tasks LLMs do well on and change the "world" (conditions/rules) while preserving the underlying reasoning procedure; the specific input-output mapping function changes. Framed causally as a do-operator/intervention on the world state (Pearl). For each (default, counterfactual) pair they add a CCC control task that tests only surface understanding of the counterfactual rules, so that a low counterfactual score can be attributed to reasoning failure rather than instruction misunderstanding. They compare default vs counterfactual accuracy across 4 closed LLMs, with and without zero-shot chain-of-thought, plus follow-up analyses (commonness of the counterfactual, few-shot ICL, logistic-regression probe of premise truthfulness effects in logic).

title: Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?
one_line: Claimed "emergent abilities" of LLMs are largely an artifact of the researcher's choice of nonlinear/discontinuous metrics (plus too-small test sets), not sharp, unpredictable new capabilities that appear at scale.
key_results: On both arithmetic tasks GPT-3 shows emergence at 4-5 digit targets under Accuracy but smooth predictable improvement under Token Edit Distance (same fixed outputs). With larger test sets, all four models score above-chance accuracy, decaying approximately geometrically with target length as the math predicts. In BIG-Bench, at most 5/39 metrics show emergence via emergence score; hand-annotated data shows emergence in only 4/39 metrics, with >92% of claimed emergent abilities under Multiple Choice Grade or Exact String Match. LaMDA emergence vanishes when swapping Multiple Choice Grade for Brier Score. They successfully induce emergence in 3 vision setups. Multiple-comparisons note: BIG-Bench has ~10^6 task-metric-model-family triplets (>=220 tasks x ~40 metrics x ~10 families).
how_it_relates: This is the foundational skeptic for the review's core question. It argues that at least a large class of apparent "new capabilities emerging at scale" are not genuinely new behaviors but measurement artifacts overlaid on smooth, already-present, gradually-improving latent competence. For the Skills/Self-Evolving debate, it supplies the cautionary methodological lesson: an apparent leap to a "new" ability can be an illusion of the metric. It implies that to claim genuine novelty (not just better elicitation of latent knowledge), one must show gains under continuous/linear metrics with adequate statistics and proper controls for multiple comparisons — otherwise a "discovery" may just be a sharp scoring rule applied to smoothly-existing capability. It does not directly measure idea-novelty; it undercuts naive claims of qualitatively new capability.
datasets: InstructGPT/GPT-3 outputs on 2-integer 2-digit multiplication (2-shot)
InstructGPT/GPT-3 outputs on 2-integer 4-digit addition (2-shot)
BIG-Bench (Srivastava et al. 2022) tasks and metrics; LaMDA outputs via BIG-Bench
Wei's 137 hand-annotated emergent abilities
CIFAR100 (autoencoder reconstruction)
Omniglot (Transformer character classification)
MNIST (LeNet CNN classification)
baselines: Wei et al. 2022 (Emergent Abilities of LLMs) claim
Ganguli et al. 2022 (Predictability and surprise)
Srivastava et al. 2022 (BIG-Bench) emergence-score methodology
Caballero et al. 2022 (Broken Neural Scaling Laws) - emergence-is-real view
Michaud et al. 2023 (Quantization model) - emergence-may-be-real view
metrics: Accuracy / subset accuracy (nonlinear)
Exact String Match (nonlinear)
Multiple Choice Grade (discontinuous)
Token Edit Distance (linear)
Brier Score (continuous, proper scoring rule)
ROUGE-L-Sum (shown to be sharp)
Per-token cross-entropy / per-token probability correct
Emergence Score (Eqn. 1, from Srivastava et al. 2022) - the paper's proxy 'discovery/emergence' metric
limitations: Analysis assumes constant per-token error probability and per-token independence (both acknowledged as empirically false, used as approximations); the power-law form is illustrative, not required. GPT analysis limited to arithmetic tasks and one publicly-queryable family; meta-analysis relies on published/available outputs (LaMDA) since most model families are not queryable. Authors explicitly do not claim LLMs cannot have emergent abilities — only that prominently claimed ones may be a mirage. Does not measure idea-level novelty/discovery; addresses capability-vs-metric, not creativity vs elicitation directly.
code: none found (paper mentions models queried via OpenAI API as of 2023-03-15 and BIG-Bench public data; no explicit code URL in main body)
evidence: The paper demonstrates that fixed model outputs, rescored under a linear metric (Token Edit Distance) instead of Accuracy, show smooth/predictable improvement — the "emergent" jump disappears without changing the model at all. On BIG-Bench, of 39 metrics at most 5 show emergence, and >92% of hand-annotated emergent abilities occur under just two metrics (Multiple Choice Grade, Exact String Match); switching LaMDA from Multiple Choice Grade to continuous Brier Score removes emergence. It further proves the point constructively by inducing brand-new "emergent" abilities in vision nets (CIFAR100 autoencoders, Omniglot Transformers, MNIST LeNet) purely by defining sharp subset-accuracy metrics. This shows underlying capability was smoothly present and merely revealed discontinuously by the metric — i.e., the "new capability" is illusory, supporting the elicitation/artifact side rather than genuine novel-capability creation. Caveat: authors explicitly do not claim LLMs can never have emergent abilities.
quotes: for a particular task and model family, when analyzing fixed model outputs, emergent abilities appear due the researcher's choice of metric rather than due to fundamental changes in model behavior with scale
emergent abilities are a mirage caused primarily by the researcher choosing a metric that nonlinearly or discontinuously deforms per-token error rates, and secondarily by possessing too few test data
we induce never-before-seen, seemingly emergent abilities in multiple architectures across various vision tasks by intentionally changing the metrics used for evaluation
contribution: Proposes and tests an alternative explanation for emergent abilities: sharp/unpredictable jumps arise because nonlinear (e.g., Accuracy/Exact-String-Match) or discontinuous (Multiple Choice Grade) metrics deform a smoothly, predictably improving per-token error rate. Shows emergence "evaporates" under linear/continuous metrics or with more test data, and even constructs never-before-seen emergent abilities in vision models by deliberately choosing sharp metrics.
methodology: A simple mathematical model assumes per-token cross-entropy falls as a power law with scale; nonlinear metrics like Accuracy = (1-eps)^L decay geometrically in target length, producing apparent sharp jumps, while linear metrics (Token Edit Distance) reveal smooth improvement. They test three predictions on the public InstructGPT/GPT-3 family (350M/1.3B/6.7B/175B) on 2-digit multiplication and 4-digit addition, a meta-analysis of BIG-Bench metrics/tasks/model families using Srivastava et al.'s "emergence score," and demonstrations inducing emergence in autoencoders (CIFAR100), causal Transformers (Omniglot), and LeNet CNNs (MNIST) via subset-accuracy metrics.

title: Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet
one_line: Without external feedback, LLMs cannot improve their reasoning by "self-correcting" and usually get worse; reported gains in prior work come from oracle labels, unfair inference-cost comparisons, or weak initial prompts rather than any genuine internal error-correction ability.
key_results: Intrinsic self-correction degrades accuracy across the board. GPT-3.5 GSM8K: 75.9 -> 75.1 -> 74.7 (rounds 1,2); CommonSenseQA: 75.8 -> 38.1 -> 41.8; HotpotQA: 26.0 -> 25.0. GPT-4 GSM8K: 95.5 -> 91.5 -> 89.0; CommonSenseQA 82.0 -> 79.5 -> 80.0; HotpotQA 49.0 -> 49.0 -> 43.0. Llama-2 GSM8K: 62.0 -> 43.5 -> 36.5; CommonSenseQA 64.0 -> 37.5 -> 36.5. With ORACLE labels, gains reappear (GPT-3.5 GSM8K 75.9 -> 84.3; CommonSenseQA 75.8 -> 89.7; GPT-4 HotpotQA 49.0 -> 59.0), confirming the improvement depends on the leaked ground truth. Transition analysis: on GSM8K GPT-3.5 changes correct->incorrect (8.8%) more than incorrect->correct (7.6%). Multi-agent debate vs self-consistency at equal budget: debate 83.2 (6 resp) vs SC 85.3 (6), debate 83.0 (9) vs SC 88.2 (9). Constrained Generation: standard prompt (theirs, full spec) 81.8 vs self-correct 67.0/75.1.
how_it_relates: Directly central and cautionary for the review's core question. It provides the strongest existing case that iterative self-improvement loops which lack an external verifier do NOT create anything new: the model cannot even reliably judge its own reasoning, so "self-correction" without a ground-truth or tool signal is at best a no-op and often harmful. It frames the improvement in prior self-correction work as an artifact of (a) oracle labels leaking the answer, (b) selection over multiple samples (self-consistency in disguise), or (c) feedback prompts that merely inject task instructions that should have been in the initial prompt, i.e., prompt-engineering effects rather than emergent capability. Implication for Skills/Self-Evolving agents: gains attributed to "self-evolution" must be checked against the possibility that they are elicitation/prompt-optimization or verifier-driven selection, not genuine novelty. The paper's flip side is also important: it argues external feedback (unit tests, calculators, trained verifiers) IS what unlocks real correction, suggesting genuine improvement requires a grounding signal outside the model's own priors.
datasets: GSM8K (1,319 grade-school math word problems; subsets of 200 for some models)
CommonSenseQA (dev set, 1,221 multiple-choice commonsense questions; subsets of 200)
HotpotQA (100 multi-hop QA questions, closed-book, exact-match)
CommonGen-Hard / Constrained Generation (20-30 concept coverage task, from Self-Refine/Madaan et al. 2023)
baselines: Standard Prompting (single initial generation)
Self-Correct with oracle labels (RCI-style, Kim et al. 2023; Reflexion, Shinn et al. 2023)
Intrinsic Self-Correction (no oracle, rounds 1-2, multiple feedback prompts)
Multi-Agent Debate (Du et al. 2023, 3 agents / 2 rounds)
Self-Consistency majority voting (Wang et al. 2022) at matched response counts
Self-Refine (Madaan et al. 2023) on Constrained Generation vs a stronger initial prompt
metrics: Accuracy / exact-match on reasoning benchmarks
Number of model calls / responses (inference-cost-matched comparison)
Answer-change transition rates: No Change, Correct->Incorrect, Incorrect->Correct, Incorrect->Incorrect
Concept coverage (for Constrained Generation)
limitations: Scope is limited to reasoning tasks; the authors note self-correction can still help for aligning style/safety/appropriateness where the model can reliably judge its output (e.g., moral self-correction), just not for detecting reasoning errors. Only two rounds of self-correction are tested. Sample sizes are small for some settings (200 per dataset, only 100 for HotpotQA, which they exclude from transition analysis as underpowered). Evaluation covers a fixed set of models (GPT-3.5/4/4-Turbo, Llama-2-70b) and specific prompts; other self-correction strategies or domains might behave differently. The positive claim that external feedback helps is cited from other work rather than newly measured here.
code: none found (no code URL given; reproducibility statement points to public OpenAI API model kernels e.g. gpt-3.5-turbo-0613, access dates, and prompts in Appendix A)
evidence: The paper demonstrates that improvements claimed for self-correction are not new capability but artifacts of information injection or sample selection. Concretely: gains vanish when oracle labels are removed (GPT-3.5 GSM8K 75.9->74.7, CommonSenseQA 75.8->41.8 after 2 rounds); multi-agent debate (83.0 at 9 responses) underperforms plain self-consistency at equal budget (88.2 at 9 responses), showing the effect is "self-consistency" via selection, not "self-correction"; and for Constrained Generation, simply putting the full requirement into the initial prompt (81.8) beats the self-corrected result (67.0/75.1), showing the apparent gain came from feedback-prompt instructions, i.e., prompt optimization. It also directly poses the paradox that if an LLM could self-correct it would just answer correctly initially, and finds "the fundamental issue is that LLMs cannot properly judge the correctness of their reasoning." This is squarely on the elicitation side: no evidence of new-idea generation, and the mechanism reframes reported wins as prompt design or selection over the model's existing answer distribution.
quotes: If an LLM possesses the ability to self-correct, why doesn't it simply offer the correct answer in its initial attempt?
The fundamental issue is that LLMs cannot properly judge the correctness of their reasoning.
The observed improvement is evidently not attributed to 'self-correction', but rather to 'self-consistency'.
contribution: A skeptical, empirical audit that debunks claims of "intrinsic self-correction" for reasoning. It (1) defines intrinsic self-correction (no external/oracle feedback), (2) shows performance drops after self-correction across GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4-Turbo, and Llama-2, (3) identifies three methodological flaws in prior work: reliance on oracle labels (RCI/Reflexion), unfair comparison of multi-agent debate vs self-consistency at equal inference cost, and sub-optimal initial prompts (Self-Refine), and (4) offers guidelines for fair future evaluation.
methodology: The authors replicate existing self-correction pipelines using a three-step prompting strategy (initial answer -> self-generated feedback -> revised answer) and compare results with vs. without oracle labels that tell the model when its answer is already correct. They also benchmark multi-agent debate against self-consistency (majority vote) at matched numbers of model responses, and re-run Self-Refine's Constrained Generation task after strengthening the initial prompt to include the full task specification. They analyze answer-change transitions (correct->incorrect vs incorrect->correct) to explain why accuracy degrades.

title: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning (Self-Taught Reasoner)
one_line: STaR bootstraps a pretrained LLM into a stronger reasoner by having it generate chain-of-thought rationales, keeping only those that yield correct answers (plus "rationalized" ones generated with the answer as a hint), and iteratively fine-tuning on its own filtered rationales.
key_results: CommonsenseQA dev: STaR with rationalization 72.5%, STaR without 68.8%, vs GPT-J Direct Finetuned 60.0%, few-shot CoT GPT-J 36.6%, few-shot direct 20.9%; comparable to 30x-larger GPT-3 fine-tuned (73.0%). Reported as +35.9% over few-shot baseline and +12.5% over direct fine-tuned. Arithmetic: 89.5% overall after 16 iterations vs 76.3% for a no-rationale baseline (5,000 steps); few-shot arithmetic accuracy is near-zero (2-digit <1%). Rationalization lifts 2-digit addition from <1% to 32% after one iteration and enables OOD 9-10 digit solving. GSM8K test: STaR with rationalization 10.7%, without 10.1%, vs GPT-J Direct Finetuned 5.8% and few-shot 3.0-3.1% — achieved using only ~25-28.7% of training data. Human eval: raters 30% more likely to prefer STaR over few-shot rationales (p=.039) and 74% more likely to prefer STaR over human rationales (p<.001).
how_it_relates: This is a strong prior-art anchor for the "elicitation" side of the question. STaR explicitly describes itself as "leveraging the LLM's pre-existing reasoning ability" and bootstrapping it — it never introduces external verification, new information, or ground truth beyond answer labels the dataset already contains. The gains come from amplifying and stabilizing latent reasoning the base model can already produce few-shot (the first iteration requires above-chance few-shot performance; GPT-2 could not bootstrap at all). It does show one narrow signal beyond pure elicitation: out-of-distribution generalization to 9-10 digit arithmetic never seen in training, and occasional novel/shorter solution paths than the human ground truth on GSM8K. But there is no metric of conceptual novelty or discovery of genuinely new ideas; "improvement" is measured purely as task accuracy and rationale-preference rankings. It supports the view that self-evolving-by-self-training largely re-weights and elicits existing capability rather than inventing new concepts, while hinting that OOD length-generalization is a modest form of going beyond the training distribution.
datasets: Arithmetic (n-digit addition with scratchpads, generated per Nye et al. 2021; 50,000 sampled questions, digits 1-5, plus OOD 9-10 digit eval)
CommonsenseQA (CQA; 9,741 train / 1,221 dev / 1,285 test, 5-way multiple choice, from ConceptNet)
GSM8K (grade-school math word problems; 7,473 train / 1,319 test)
baselines: Few-shot Direct GPT-J (no rationale)
Few-shot CoT GPT-J (with rationale)
GPT-J Direct Finetuned (fine-tuned to predict answer directly, 100% of data)
GPT-3 Direct Finetuned (73.0% on CQA, ~30x larger)
Few-shot CoT LaMDA 137B
STaR without rationalization (ablation)
Higher-temperature sampling as an alternative to rationalization (found counterproductive)
metrics: Task accuracy (arithmetic per-digit and overall, CQA dev accuracy, GSM8K test accuracy)
Fraction of training data covered / used
Human preference ranking of rationale quality (crowdworkers on Prolific)
Agreement between generated vs human calculator-step counts (GSM8K)
OOD accuracy on unseen digit lengths (9-10 digit addition) - no dedicated novelty/discovery metric
limitations: First iteration requires above-chance few-shot performance, so the base model must already possess reasoning ability (GPT-2 could not bootstrap even on arithmetic) — improvement is gated by pre-existing capability. High-chance settings (e.g. binary decisions) yield many correct answers with bad reasoning, confounding the filter (open problem: how to filter bad reasoning). Rationalization requires a way to inject a "hint," which may be nontrivial in some domains. Higher-temperature sampling as an alternative degrades reasoning (scratchpads "diverge into meaninglessness"). No verification of rationale validity beyond final-answer correctness, so correct-answer-wrong-reasoning traces can leak in. No metric of conceptual novelty; limited compute prevented hyperparameter search. Rationale quality on CQA is only qualitatively/human-judged.
code: Uses publicly available GPT-J 6B checkpoint and fine-tuning script from the GPT-J repository (ref [26]); no dedicated STaR code/data URL stated in the read pages.
evidence: The paper's own framing and design place it on the elicitation side: it "leverages the LLM's pre-existing reasoning ability" and requires the base model to already have above-chance few-shot reasoning (GPT-2 fails to bootstrap; the initial model "must be big enough to have some reasoning capabilities"). No new external knowledge or verifier is added — only answer labels already present in standard datasets — so the loop redistributes probability mass toward reasoning paths the model can already sample, rather than acquiring new facts or inventing new mechanisms. The mathematical framing as a policy-gradient over latent rationales the model itself proposes reinforces that this is amplification of latent ability. Counter-evidence for pure elicitation: successful OOD generalization to 9-10 digit sums never seen in training, and occasional discovery of shorter-than-human solutions on GSM8K (skipping redundant steps). But no novelty/discovery metric is defined; evaluation is task accuracy plus human preference rankings of rationale quality. Human evaluators preferred STaR rationales over human-written ones 74% of the time, which the authors caution is about the difficulty of "eliciting high-quality rationales," not human-level reasoning — again framing the contribution as elicitation.
quotes: In this paper, we adopt a different approach: by leveraging the LLM's pre-existing reasoning ability, we iteratively bootstrap the ability to generate high-quality rationales.

In order for the first iteration of STaR to succeed, few-shot performance must be above chance, implying that the initial model must be big enough to have some reasoning capabilities. For instance we found that GPT-2 was not able to bootstrap from few-shot reasoning in even the arithmetic domain.
when evaluated on 9 and 10 digit examples, never seen during training, the model successfully solves many of these out-of-distribution problems.
contribution: Introduces a self-improvement loop that turns ~10 few-shot rationale examples plus a large answer-labeled (but rationale-free) dataset into a large self-generated rationale training set, without needing to verify rationale correctness (correctness of the final answer is the filter). Adds "rationalization" (reasoning backward from the given correct answer) to obtain training signal on problems the model initially fails. Claimed as, to their knowledge, the first technique letting a pretrained LLM iteratively use its own language-modeling capacity to improve itself.
methodology: Few-shot prompt the LLM (GPT-J, 6B) to produce a rationale then an answer; filter to rationales whose final answer equals ground truth; for failed problems, prompt again with the correct answer supplied as a hint ("rationalization") and keep those that now reach the correct answer (with the hint stripped from the stored prompt); fine-tune the original pretrained model on the union of correct + rationalized-correct rationales; repeat, regenerating data each outer loop from the improved model. The authors frame this as an approximation to an RL policy-gradient objective where the indicator 1(y_hat = y) reward implements the filtering step, with greedy (low-temperature) decoding to reduce variance.

title: The Red Queen Gödel Machine: Co-Evolving Agents and Their Evaluators
one_line: A recursive self-improvement framework (RQGM) in which learned evaluators co-evolve with the task agents they score, using "controlled utility evolution" (epoch-based frozen evaluators swapped at boundaries against a ground-truth anchor) to make non-stationary evaluation compatible with prior self-improvement guarantees.
key_results: Coding (Polyglot): RQGM reaches 71.7% held-out pass rate vs prior SOTA HGM-H 69.9%, using 1.35x-1.72x fewer search tokens by adding a cheap agent-as-a-judge code reviewer; ~90% of accepted patches modify shared task-agent/infrastructure code benefiting both roles. Paper writing (Tab. 1): co-evolved writer raises mean reviewer-panel acceptance from HGM-H's 21.8% to 38.8% at matched compute (1.78x) and 40.5% for the best specialist (1.86x). Proof grading (Fig. 5): co-evolved RQGM grader reaches best IMO-GradingBench accuracy (~75-76%) at 3x lower search cost than HGM-H; grader ~9% higher ground-truth accuracy per abstract. Proof writing (Tab. 2): RQGM specialist prover Score 4.33 vs IMO25 4.07 and HGM-H 3.73, Pass@6 61.7% vs 55.0%/51.7%, but Pass@7 48.3% below static IMO25's 55.0% (wins by finding near-complete 6/7 proofs). Debiasing (Fig. 6): baseline HGM-H reviewer over-accepts AI papers at 1.42x-1.91x the human rate; adversarial RQGM reviewer accepts AI and human papers at similar rates while retaining ~80% ground-truth accuracy. Hybrid model (Fig. 7): routing task-agent calls through Nemotron 3 Ultra approaches GPT-5.5-only accuracy at ~13x lower price-equivalent search cost.
how_it_relates: This paper is squarely about the "moving-goalpost verification problem": what happens when the evaluator itself is learned and evolves alongside the agent. It is highly relevant to the elicitation-vs-novelty question because it directly confronts reward hacking and the limits of static benchmarks, yet its own honest framing is that genuine improvement is anchored to (and therefore bounded by) fixed ground-truth data. The authors explicitly state that anchoring "confines them to the anchor's own decision boundary" and that going "far beyond the benchmark must therefore come from the objectives layered on top of the ground-truth signal." Their one gesture toward creating something NOT specified by the benchmark is the adversarial reviewer that "discovers a human-machine decision boundary the benchmark never specified" — but this is a debiasing/calibration move, not invention of a genuinely new concept. All demonstrated gains (pass rate, acceptance rate, grading accuracy) are measured against pre-existing human/benchmark targets, and the mechanism is search over LLM-generated code/agent variants scored by LLM judges — i.e., better elicitation and orchestration of latent capability, not evidence of de-novo idea creation.
datasets: Polyglot (aider coding benchmark, executable tests)
CRAVE (accepted/rejected pull-request dataset; anchor for the code reviewer)
APReS (agentic paper revision & evaluation system; accept/reject decisions; anchor for paper review; matching writer dataset of titles/abstracts constructed)
IMO-GradingBench (human grades for Olympiad proofs; anchor for proof grading)
SWE-bench (explicitly EXCLUDED due to long runtime)
baselines: HGM-H (Huxley-Gödel Machine search substituted into HyperAgents, evaluators kept frozen with fixed external evaluator) — primary baseline in every domain
Sakana prompt reviewer (fixed external reviewer for paper writing)
ProofAutoGrader (fixed external grader for proof grading)
IMO25 prover (Huang & Yang human-engineered verification-and-refinement pipeline, gold-medal IMO 2025)
DGM-H (best published HyperAgents patches, proxy for Darwin-Gödel-Machine-level performance, on paper reviewing and grading)
metrics: Held-out test pass rate (coding)
Blended search tokens = input + 5x output (efficiency / cost-to-quality)
epsilon-best-belief utility (epsilon=0.05; Beta-posterior lower bound)
Reviewer-panel acceptance rate
IMO-GradingBench grading accuracy and normalized grading MAE
Proof Score (mean 0-7), Pass@6, Pass@7
Spearman rho of post- vs pre-replacement archive rankings (curriculum / re-ranking measure)
AI-vs-human paper acceptance-rate ratio (self-preference bias / calibration)
NOTE: no explicit novelty or discovery metric is used
limitations: Authors call it a "preliminary empirical investigation" over "short search horizons" they intend to extend. Two explicit limitations: (1) evaluator quality is only as good as its anchor — a weak or biased ground-truth anchor yields uninformative evaluators; (2) theoretical guarantees are epoch-LOCAL only, covering improvement within an epoch, not globally across epochs (loosening convergence guarantees vs static evaluation). SWE-bench excluded for runtime. Proof domain gains only emerge at longer horizons and still lose on the strict Pass@7 metric. No novelty/discovery metric; all gains measured against fixed human/benchmark targets. Improvement "far beyond the benchmark" is deferred to future work.
code: none found
evidence: The paper demonstrates efficiency and quality gains via co-evolved evaluators, but every headline metric is agreement with a fixed external target (Polyglot test pass rate, IMO-GradingBench human grades, APReS accept/reject decisions, reviewer-panel acceptance). It offers no novelty/discovery metric and no claim of inventing a new concept; improvements are re-rankings of LLM-generated agent variants under progressively stricter LLM-as-judge criteria. The authors themselves cap the ceiling: evaluators are "only as good as their anchor," anchoring "confines them to the anchor's own decision boundary," and any capability "beyond the reach of static benchmarks" is deferred to future work with richer layered objectives. The single case going beyond the benchmark (the adversarial anti-self-preference reviewer) is calibration/debiasing rather than novel-idea generation. Hence the work is a better way to exploit/orchestrate latent LLM capability under moving evaluators, not demonstrated evidence of genuine novelty — but it is neutral/mixed because it explicitly identifies bootstrapping "beyond the reach of static benchmarks" as the goal and provides the epoch/anchor machinery as a step toward it.
quotes: We believe the RQGM points toward a class of self-improving co-evolving systems in which agents and evaluators recursively bootstrap one another without further human intervention to reach capabilities beyond those of static evaluation ... at the cost of loosening convergence guarantees compared to static evaluation criteria.

The limit of this improvement is governed by the anchor. Anchoring every replacement to fixed ground truth keeps evaluators accurate but confines them to the anchor's own decision boundary. Improvement far beyond the benchmark must therefore come from the objectives layered on top of the ground-truth signal, with the anchor serving as a guardrail against drift.

The adversarial reviewer is the first such instance: it roughly maintains accuracy on the ground truth while the adversarial term discovers a human-machine decision boundary the benchmark never specified.
contribution: Three stated contributions: (1) recursive self-improvement under NON-stationary utilities by treating the evaluation signal as part of the search; (2) "controlled utility evolution" that keeps prior Gödel-Machine/HGM self-improvement guarantees per-epoch — search runs in fixed-evaluator epochs, and a challenger evaluator is promoted only when it raises an epsilon-best-belief score on a fixed ground-truth anchor; (3) empirical demonstrations that co-evolved learned evaluators add a cheap code-review signal (improving efficiency even where a verifier exists), enable generator search where no benchmark exists (paper/proof writing), and enable debiasing over-lenient evaluators via adversarial objectives at epoch boundaries.
methodology: Builds on tree-search self-improvement (Darwin-Gödel Machine, Huxley-Gödel Machine / HGM, HyperAgents): an archive of agent nodes grown by a meta-agent that self-modifies code, with nodes selected via Thompson sampling over Clade MetaProductivity (CMP) and the final agent chosen by an epsilon-best-belief (Beta-posterior lower-bound) score. RQGM's four changes: each node is a multi-agent shared workspace (task-agent + evaluator roles, editable code) rather than a single agent; evaluators are themselves learned agentic processes; utilities may change at "evolutionary epoch" boundaries; and evaluators may be replaced there. Within an epoch the evaluator is frozen (stationary utility); at a checkpoint (exponentially spaced) a challenger evaluator replaces the incumbent only if it statistically outperforms it on a held-out ground-truth anchor, after which "selective erasure" discards only the utility records tied to the displaced evaluator. This makes each epoch a fixed-criterion search so HGM guarantees apply per-epoch; theory (Props 3-6) bounds re-evaluation cost to O(B).
